Travel & Expense Policy — Overview
Document:  TEP-001 Version:  3.2 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Finance Operations 
Applies To:  All Northwind Logistics employees, contractors, and authorized agents
1. Purpose
This policy establishes the rules and procedures governing business travel and the reimbursement of
business expenses incurred by employees of Northwind Logistics ("the Company"). All employees are
responsible for understanding and complying with this policy.
2. Scope
This policy applies to all expenses incurred in the course of business travel, client entertainment,
conference attendance, and ordinary business operations where Company funds are committed or
reimbursed.
3. General principles
3.1. Business purpose.  Every reimbursable expense must have a clear and documented business
purpose. The Company does not reimburse personal expenses under any circumstances.
3.2. Reasonableness.  Expenses must be reasonable, necessary, and incurred at the lowest practical
cost consistent with effective business performance.
3.3. Documentation.  All expenses must be supported by original itemized receipts as detailed in the
Receipt Requirements Policy (TEP-007).
3.4. Timeliness.  Expense reports must be submitted within 30 days of the end of the trip. Expenses
submitted more than 60 days after they were incurred will not be reimbursed except by VP-level
exception.
4. Approval workflow
4.1. Expenses up to $1,000 per submission: direct manager approval.
4.2. Expenses $1,000–$5,000: Director approval required.
4.3. Expenses above $5,000: VP approval required.
4.4. Approval thresholds are cumulative per submission, not per line item.

5. Related policies
This policy is supplemented by the following, each of which controls in the area of its subject matter:
TEP-002 Meals and Entertainment Policy
TEP-003 Alcohol Policy
TEP-004 Lodging Policy
TEP-005 Air Travel Policy
TEP-006 Ground Transportation Policy
TEP-007 Receipt Requirements Policy
TEP-008 Per-Diem Rates
TEP-009 Employee Grades Reference
TEP-010 Corporate Card Policy
TEP-013 International Travel Policy
TEP-014 Conference Attendance Policy
6. Audit and enforcement
6.1. Finance Operations conducts random and risk-based audits of submitted expense reports.
Selected submissions will require additional documentation.
6.2. Violations of this policy may result in non-reimbursement, requirement to repay, or disciplinary
action up to and including termination, depending on severity and intent.
6.3. Repeat or willful violations are escalated to HR and the relevant business unit head.
7. Exceptions
Exceptions to this policy require written approval from the CFO or designated delegate prior to
incurring the expense. Retroactive exception requests are strongly discouraged and granted only in
genuine emergencies.
8. Definitions
For purposes of this policy and the broader Travel & Expense framework:
Reimbursable  — Eligible for repayment by the Company subject to compliance with
applicable policy and supporting documentation.
VP approval  — Written approval from a Grade 9 employee or above per TEP-009 §3.2. Email is
sufficient unless otherwise specified.
Approved booking tool  — Concur Travel, the Company's contracted travel booking platform.
Corporate Card  — A payment card issued in the employee's name under the Corporate Card
Policy (TEP-010).•

Submission  — An expense report submitted via Concur or successor system, comprising one
or more line items each supported by appropriate documentation.
Audit  — Random or risk-based review of submitted expense reports by Finance Operations,
supplemented by external audit as required.

Meals and Entertainment Policy
Document:  TEP-002 Version:  2.4 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Finance Operations
1. Scope
This policy governs the reimbursement of meal expenses incurred during business travel, in-office
working meals, and client entertainment. Alcohol-related rules are addressed separately in TEP-003
(Alcohol Policy).
2. Per-meal caps for individual business travel
Meal expenses incurred by an employee traveling on business, when dining alone or with other
Northwind employees, are subject to the following per-person caps (inclusive of tax and tip):
Meal Cap (USD)
Breakfast $25
Lunch $35
Dinner $75
2.1. The caps in Section 2 apply per person per meal. Multiple meals stacked under one receipt (e.g.,
a brunch combining breakfast and lunch) are subject to the higher applicable cap.
2.2. Expenses above these caps are not reimbursable except under Section 4 (Client Entertainment)
or Section 5 (Exceptions).
2.3. High-cost cities.  Caps are increased by 25% for travel in Tier 1 cities (see Lodging Policy
TEP-004 §3 for the current Tier 1 list).
3. Tips
Tips above 20% of the pre-tax meal total are considered personal and are not reimbursable.
4. Client entertainment
4.1. Meal expenses incurred while hosting clients, prospects, or external partners are governed by
separate caps:
Lunch: up to $80 per person
Dinner: up to $150 per person• 
•

4.2. Client entertainment expenses above  $100 per person require prior written approval from a Vice
President or higher.
4.3. The employee must record on the receipt or expense report:
Names of all attendees
The business purpose of the meeting
The name of the company or organization represented by each external attendee
4.4. Entertainment of family members, friends, or non-business contacts is never reimbursable,
regardless of who else is present.
5. Exceptions to per-meal caps
5.1. Where a meal is part of a conference, training, or off-site whose registration fee includes meals,
the registration fee covers the meal and no separate meal reimbursement is permitted.
5.2. Where a meal is unavailable at or below the cap due to extraordinary circumstances (e.g., the
only open restaurant in a small town has a $90 dinner menu), the employee may submit the actual
cost with written explanation; reimbursement requires manager approval.
5.3. Standing exceptions are not granted. Each exception is reviewed individually.
6. Non-reimbursable categories
The following are never reimbursable as meal expenses:
Alcoholic beverages on solo travel (see TEP-003)
Mini-bar items from hotel rooms
Meals for accompanying spouses, family members, or guests not on company business
"Recovery" or "team morale" meals not pre-approved by manager
7. Frequently asked questions
7.1. "My breakfast at the hotel was charged as $32 because of a 'destination resort breakfast' fee. Is
this reimbursable?"  The mandatory portion bundled into a daily resort fee is reimbursable as part of
the room rate (see TEP-004 §4.2). The breakfast itself counts against the breakfast cap; reasonable
overages within the resort context may be approved by your manager.
7.2. "I attended a team off-site where one Northwind colleague is technically a contractor. Does that
count as client entertainment?"  No. Contractors paid by the Company are not external clients for
purposes of this policy. Apply standard per-meal caps.
7.3. "The check arrived late and I tipped the server in cash beyond what's on the receipt.

Contractors paid by the Company are not external clients for
purposes of this policy. Apply standard per-meal caps.
7.3. "The check arrived late and I tipped the server in cash beyond what's on the receipt. Is the cash
tip reimbursable?"  The tip must be reflected on the receipt or submitted with a Missing Receipt• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
•

Affidavit (TEP-007 §4.2) describing the cash portion. Reimbursement remains capped at 20% per
Section 3.
7.4. "My client picked up the check. Can I expense my entertainment of them at our next meeting
instead?"  Yes, subject to the standard caps and approval requirements. Each entertainment event is
evaluated independently.
8. Definitions
Meal  — Breakfast, lunch, or dinner consumed in the course of business travel or business
activity. Snacks, coffees, and small purchases under $10 are not "meals" for cap purposes and
are tracked as incidentals.
Client entertainment  — A meal or event hosted by a Company employee for one or more
external clients, prospects, or business partners, with the primary purpose of relationship
development.
Itemized receipt  — A receipt showing each individual item or charge, as defined in TEP-007
§3.
High-cost city  — A city in Lodging Tier 1 per TEP-004 §3. The current Tier 1 list is the
controlling list for meal-cap purposes.

Alcohol Policy
Document:  TEP-003 Version:  1.8 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Finance Operations, in
coordination with Human Resources
1. Scope
This policy governs the reimbursement of alcoholic beverages charged to the Company, whether on a
Corporate Card, personal card with reimbursement request, or in any other form.
2. General rule
Alcoholic beverages are reimbursable only when incurred during sanctioned client
entertainment.
2.1. "Sanctioned client entertainment" means a meal or event hosted by a Northwind employee for
one or more external clients, prospects, or business partners, where the primary purpose is the
development or maintenance of a business relationship.
2.2. Sanctioned client entertainment involving alcohol requires:
At least one external attendee to be physically present
Prior approval from a Vice President or higher, documented in writing (email is sufficient)
A per-person alcohol spend that does not exceed $50
3. Prohibited charges
The following alcohol-related charges are never reimbursable , even if charged on the Corporate
Card:
3.1. Solo travel.  Any alcoholic beverage purchased while traveling on business without external
clients present. This includes drinks at hotel bars, with meals taken alone, or at airport lounges,
regardless of amount.
3.2. Team-only meals.  Alcohol consumed at meals attended only by Northwind employees, including
offsite dinners, holiday gatherings (unless explicitly designated as a Company event with HR
approval), and team morale events.
3.3. In-room consumption.  Alcohol purchased through hotel room service or mini-bars under any
circumstances.
3.4. Excess of cap.  The portion of any alcohol charge exceeding the $50 per-person cap defined in
Section 2.2.• 
• 
•

3.5. Personal consumption disguised as a meal.  Alcohol charges where the food portion of the
receipt is minimal (less than 50% of the receipt total) will be reviewed for compliance and may be
denied.
4. Reporting requirements
4.1. When alcohol is included on a reimbursable receipt, the alcohol portion must be itemized
separately on the expense report, even when bundled with food on the same receipt.
4.2. The expense report must identify:
All attendees (employees and externals)
The VP-level approver for the entertainment
The business purpose
5. Non-reimbursement and recovery
5.1. If a Corporate Card charge for alcohol is found to violate this policy in audit, the employee is
responsible for repaying the Company within 30 days of notification.
5.2. Repeated violations are referred to HR for disciplinary review.
6. Local law
In jurisdictions where alcohol consumption or purchase is restricted by law (e.g., dry counties, certain
international destinations), this policy yields to local law and no alcohol expenses will be reimbursed
regardless of business context.
7. Examples and edge cases
7.1. Solo dinner with a beer.  Employee A on a solo trip to Denver orders a $58 dinner including one
$9 beer. The $9 alcohol charge is not reimbursable per §3.1. The $49 food portion remains
reimbursable subject to TEP-002 §2 caps. The line item should be flagged and only the food portion
reimbursed.
7.2. Working dinner with two Northwind colleagues.  Three Northwind employees dine together
while traveling for the same project. Alcohol is not reimbursable per §3.2 (team-only meal). The food
portion is reimbursable subject to per-person caps in TEP-002 §2.
7.3. Pre-arranged client entertainment.  A Director and a Senior Manager host two external
customers for dinner with prior VP approval documented by email. Alcohol per person is reimbursable
up to $50, food up to the entertainment cap in TEP-002 §4. Receipt must itemize alcohol separately
per §4.1.• 
• 
•

7.4. Conference attendee reception.  A conference reception included drinks as part of the
registration. No incremental alcohol charge to the Company occurs; the registration fee is
reimbursable under TEP-014.
7.5. Hotel mini-bar wine.  Never reimbursable, regardless of business context (§3.3).
8. Definitions
Alcohol  — Any beverage containing ethyl alcohol at a concentration greater than 0.5% by
volume. Includes beer, wine, spirits, hard seltzer, and prepared cocktails.
Sanctioned client entertainment  — As defined in §2.1.
External attendee  — A person who is not a current employee of Northwind Logistics or its
affiliates. Contractors and consultants paid by the Company are not external attendees.
Itemization  — A receipt showing alcohol charges separately from food charges, sufficient to
identify the alcohol portion as a discrete amount.

Lodging Policy
Document:  TEP-004 Version:  2.1 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Finance Operations
1. Scope
This policy governs the reimbursement of lodging expenses (hotels, short-term rentals, and
equivalent) incurred during business travel.
2. Booking requirements
2.1. Lodging should be booked through the Company's approved booking tool (Concur Travel)
whenever practical. Bookings outside the tool require manager approval and a brief justification.
2.2. Employees should book lodging at least 14 days in advance whenever the trip is planned that far
ahead. Last-minute bookings (under 7 days) require manager approval if the rate exceeds the
applicable cap.
3. City tier rate caps
Maximum reimbursable nightly rate (room rate plus mandatory taxes and resort fees; excludes
incidentals):
Tier 1 — $350 per night
New York, NY; San Francisco, CA; Boston, MA; Washington, DC; Los Angeles, CA; Seattle, WA;
London, UK; Zurich, CH; Tokyo, JP; Singapore, SG.
Tier 2 — $250 per night
Chicago, IL; Denver, CO; Atlanta, GA; Austin, TX; Dallas, TX; Houston, TX; Miami, FL; Portland, OR;
San Diego, CA; Toronto, ON; Amsterdam, NL; Berlin, DE; Sydney, AU.
Tier 3 — $175 per night
All other locations within the United States and Canada.
Tier 4 — Submit for approval
International destinations not listed above. Rate cap is set at the time of booking based on prevailing
local rates and approved by Finance Operations.

4. Room category
4.1. Standard room or equivalent  is the default category. Suites, club-level rooms, and view
upgrades are not reimbursable unless they are the lowest available rate in the property or the upgrade
is offered at no additional charge.
4.2. Short-term rentals  (Airbnb, VRBO) are permitted at the applicable tier cap. Cleaning fees and
service fees count toward the cap.
5. Incidentals not reimbursable
The following are never reimbursable as lodging:
Mini-bar consumption (food or alcohol)
In-room movies, video games, or other entertainment
Spa, gym, or recreation surcharges (gym is reimbursable only if the hotel has a mandatory daily
resort/gym fee, in which case it counts toward the room cap)
Laundry, except for trips exceeding 5 consecutive nights
Personal phone calls
6. Exceeding the cap
6.1. Where the city tier cap cannot be met at any available property (e.g., a conference has driven up
rates citywide), the employee may submit the actual cost with manager approval and a screenshot or
written explanation showing comparable rates.
6.2. Standing exceptions are not granted.
7. Cancellation and no-show charges
7.1. Cancellation charges for legitimate business reasons (trip cancelled by manager, illness, family
emergency) are reimbursable.
7.2. No-show or late-cancellation charges due to employee oversight are not reimbursable.
8. Edge cases and worked examples
8.1. Tier 1 cap exceeded due to citywide event.  A conference in San Francisco has driven hotel
rates citywide to $480/nt. The Tier 1 cap is $350/nt. The employee may book the lowest available rate
and submit screenshots of comparable rates with manager approval, per §6.1. Without that
supporting documentation, the excess over $350/nt is not reimbursable.
8.2. Tier 2 city, premium hotel selected for convenience.  Employee books the Hyatt Regency
Chicago at $315/nt; cap is $250. Excess is not reimbursable unless a lower-cost alternative was• 
• 
• 
• 
•

demonstrably unavailable. Manager should encourage selection within cap absent an unusual
circumstance.
8.3. Tier 1 city, room with view upgrade auto-applied.  Employee did not request an upgrade but
received one at check-in (no additional charge). Reimbursable as the lowest available rate.
8.4. Short-term rental.  Employee selects an Airbnb at $190/nt + $75 cleaning fee + $20 service fee =
$285 for one night in Atlanta (Tier 2, cap $250). The cleaning and service fees count toward the cap
per §4.2. The submission exceeds the cap by $35 and is flagged.
8.5. Mandatory resort fee.  Hotel bills $20/nt mandatory resort fee that includes Wi-Fi and gym. This
counts toward the cap per §3 (rate caps include mandatory taxes and resort fees).
9. Definitions
Nightly rate  — The base room rate plus all mandatory taxes, occupancy fees, tourism
assessments, and resort/destination fees, divided by nights stayed. Optional incidentals
(parking, laundry beyond §5 exceptions, mini-bar) are not part of the nightly rate.
Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3 / Tier 4  — City classifications per §3, used for lodging rate caps and
per-diem rates.
Concur Travel  — The Company's approved booking tool. References to "approved booking
tool" mean Concur Travel.
Short-term rental  — Lodging provided by Airbnb, VRBO, or equivalent platform.

Air Travel Policy
Document:  TEP-005 Version:  2.0 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Finance Operations
1. Scope
This policy governs the reimbursement of air travel expenses (commercial passenger flights) for
business purposes.
2. Class of service
2.1. Economy class  is the default for all domestic and short-haul international flights. Domestic
flights are flights wholly within the contiguous United States, Alaska, or Hawaii.
2.2. Premium economy  (or equivalent) is permitted on any single flight segment with a scheduled
duration of 6 hours or more. The premium economy upgrade does not require additional approval if
the flight meets the duration threshold.
2.3. Business class  is permitted only on international flight segments with a scheduled duration of 10
hours or more, and requires prior written approval from a Vice President or higher.
2.4. First class  is not reimbursable under any circumstances.
3. Booking requirements
3.1. Flights should be booked through Concur Travel whenever practical.
3.2. Flights should be booked at least 14 days in advance whenever the trip is planned that far ahead.
Last-minute bookings (under 7 days) require manager approval.
3.3. Employees should book the lowest reasonable fare consistent with the business need,
considering:
Direct flights are preferred when the price difference is less than $200 vs a one-stop itinerary
Schedule constraints (cannot arrive after 11pm the night before a morning meeting)
Departure flexibility (avoid red-eyes unless required by the business)
4. Ancillary fees
4.1. Checked baggage.  First checked bag is reimbursable. Second checked bag requires manager
approval and a business justification.• 
• 
•

4.2. Seat selection.  Standard seat selection (window/aisle/middle) is reimbursable. Extra-legroom
seats are reimbursable for flights of 4 hours or more, or for employees with a documented
accommodation need.
4.3. Change and cancellation fees  are reimbursable when the change is for a documented business
reason. Changes for personal preference are not reimbursable.
4.4. In-flight Wi-Fi  is reimbursable if used for business purposes.
5. Loyalty programs
5.1. Employees may retain frequent-flyer miles and points earned on business travel.
5.2. Employees may not select higher-cost flights solely to accumulate miles. The lowest reasonable
fare governs.
6. Travel with companions
6.1. Travel costs for spouses, family members, or other companions are never reimbursable, even
when they accompany the employee on a business trip.
6.2. If a business itinerary is extended for personal reasons (e.g., a weekend before or after meetings),
only the airfare cost that would have been incurred for the business portion is reimbursable.
Employees should obtain a comparison quote at booking time.
7. Definitions
Domestic flight  — A flight wholly within the contiguous United States, Alaska, or Hawaii.
Short-haul international  — An international flight segment with scheduled duration less than 6
hours.
Long-haul international  — An international flight segment with scheduled duration 6 hours or
more.
Class of service  — The fare class booked: Economy (Main Cabin / Coach), Premium Economy
(Comfort+, Economy Plus, Premium Select, etc.), Business, or First. Names vary by carrier; the
class category controls.
Comparison quote  — A screenshot or saved itinerary showing the alternative fare that would
have been incurred without the personal extension or upgrade.

Ground Transportation Policy
Document:  TEP-006 Version:  1.6 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Finance Operations
1. Scope
This policy governs the reimbursement of ground transportation expenses incurred during business
travel, including rideshare, taxis, rental cars, public transit, personal vehicle mileage, and parking.
2. Rideshare and taxi
2.1. Rideshare services (Uber, Lyft, and regional equivalents) are reimbursable for business-related
transportation, including airport transfers, transit between meetings, and end-of-day return to lodging.
2.2. Standard service categories (UberX, Lyft Standard, regional equivalents) should be selected.
Premium categories (Uber Black, Lyft Lux, etc.) are not reimbursable unless they are the only available
option, in which case a written explanation is required.
2.3. Taxis are reimbursable at the metered fare plus reasonable tip (up to 20% of the fare).
3. Rental cars
3.1. Rental cars are appropriate where rideshare or taxi is impractical (e.g., multiple stops over
multiple days, rural destinations, multiple-passenger transport).
3.2. Vehicle category.  Compact or mid-size vehicles are the default. Full-size vehicles are
reimbursable only when transporting 3 or more passengers or significant equipment. Premium, luxury,
and SUV categories are not reimbursable unless required by terrain or capacity, with manager
approval.
3.3. Insurance.  Decline collision damage waiver (CDW) and liability insurance offered by the rental
company when traveling within the United States — the Company's commercial auto policy covers
these. Outside the United States, accept the rental company's CDW.
3.4. Fuel.  Refuel before returning the vehicle. Pre-paid fuel options and per-gallon refueling charges
from the rental company are not reimbursable.
4. Personal vehicle mileage
4.1. Use of a personal vehicle for business purposes is reimbursed at the IRS standard mileage rate,
currently $0.67 per mile  for 2025.

4.2. Reimbursement covers business mileage only. Commuting mileage (home to regular office) is not
reimbursable.
4.3. Mileage must be documented with starting point, destination, business purpose, and total miles
for each trip.
5. Public transit
5.1. Public transit (subway, bus, light rail, commuter rail) is reimbursable at actual cost.
5.2. Multi-day or weekly passes are reimbursable if they are economically equivalent to or cheaper
than the sum of individual fares for the business travel period.
6. Parking
6.1. Parking at business destinations, hotels, and airports during business travel is reimbursable at
actual cost.
6.2. Long-term airport parking should be used for trips of 3 days or more where the cost is lower than
ground transportation alternatives.
6.3. Valet parking is reimbursable only where it is the only available option or where the price
difference vs self-parking is under $15.
7. Tolls
Highway tolls incurred during business travel are reimbursable at actual cost.
8. Definitions
Rideshare  — A peer-to-peer ground transportation service such as Uber, Lyft, or regional
equivalent (Grab, Bolt, Didi, etc.).
Standard service category  — The base service tier offered by the rideshare provider (UberX,
Lyft Standard, etc.), as opposed to premium tiers.
IRS standard mileage rate  — The per-mile rate published annually by the U.S. Internal
Revenue Service for business use of a personal vehicle. The current rate is in §4.1; it is updated
by addendum when the IRS publishes a change.
Mid-size vehicle  — A rental car category between compact and full-size, typically seating 5
adults.
Commuting mileage  — Travel between the employee's home and their regular office, which is
not reimbursable.• 
• 
• 
• 
•

9. Related documents

Receipt Requirements Policy
Document:  TEP-007 Version:  1.5 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Finance Operations
1. General rule
All expenses claimed for reimbursement must be supported by an original receipt, with the limited
exceptions in Section 4.
2. Required receipt content
A valid receipt must show, at minimum:
Vendor / merchant name
Date of the transaction
Total amount and currency
Itemized breakdown of charges (for meals and entertainment, see Section 3)
Method of payment (last four digits of card sufficient; cash receipts require vendor stamp or
signature)
3. Itemized receipts for meals and entertainment
3.1. Meal and entertainment expenses require an itemized receipt  showing each item ordered, in
addition to the summary tip-and-total receipt.
3.2. Where a vendor does not provide an itemized receipt (e.g., a food truck with hand-written totals
only), the employee must record an itemization on the expense report.
3.3. Itemization is necessary to verify compliance with the Meals & Entertainment Policy (TEP-002)
and the Alcohol Policy (TEP-003), specifically the requirement that alcohol be itemized separately.
4. Receipt waivers
4.1. Under-$25 threshold.  Individual expenses of less than $25 do not require a receipt, except for
the following categories which require receipts regardless of amount: any alcohol charge, any tip
exceeding $20, any cash advance disbursement.
4.2. Lost receipts.  If a receipt is lost or unavailable, the employee may submit a Missing Receipt
Affidavit (available in Concur) attesting to the expense. Affidavits may not be used more than twice
per quarter per employee.• 
• 
• 
• 
•

4.3. Per-diem travel.  When traveling on a per-diem basis (see TEP-008), individual meal receipts are
not required for meals covered by the per-diem.
5. Receipt format
5.1. Electronic receipts (email confirmations, PDF receipts, mobile receipt apps) are acceptable.
5.2. Photographs of paper receipts taken with a mobile camera are acceptable provided all required
content is clearly legible.
5.3. Translated receipts: receipts in languages other than English do not require translation, but the
expense report line description must be in English.
6. Receipt matching
6.1. Each receipt submitted must match the line item it supports. The amount on the receipt must
match the amount claimed (subject to small rounding differences of under $1 due to currency
conversion).
6.2. Amount mismatch.  Where the receipt amount differs materially from the amount claimed, the
line item will be flagged. Reimbursement is the lesser of the two unless the employee provides a
satisfactory explanation (e.g., currency conversion, separate gratuity charge).
6.3. Receipt-line orphans.  Line items without a supporting receipt (other than those covered by
Section 4) will be flagged and may be non-reimbursable.
7. Retention
The Company retains submitted receipts for 7 years per applicable tax and audit requirements.
Employees should retain a personal copy of any unusual or high-value receipts for the same period.
8. Definitions
Receipt  — A document issued by a vendor at the time of transaction evidencing payment.
Itemized receipt  — A receipt showing each individual item or charge separately, as opposed
to a summary-total receipt.
Missing Receipt Affidavit  — A standard form (available in Concur) by which an employee
attests to an expense for which no receipt is available.
Currency conversion  — Translation of foreign-currency amounts to U.S. dollars at the rate
posted by the Corporate Card issuer or, where not posted, the OANDA daily rate on the
transaction date.• 
• 
• 
•

Per-Diem Rates
Document:  TEP-008 Version:  1.3 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Finance Operations
1. Scope
This policy establishes the per-diem rates available to employees on extended business travel, as an
alternative to itemized meal reimbursement.
2. Eligibility
2.1. Per-diem is an opt-in  alternative for trips lasting 3 or more consecutive nights  away from the
employee's home base. Trips shorter than 3 nights must use itemized meal reimbursement under
TEP-002.
2.2. The choice between per-diem and itemized must be declared at the start of the trip and may not
be changed mid-trip.
2.3. Per-diem covers meals and incidentals only. Lodging is reimbursed separately at actual cost per
the Lodging Policy (TEP-004), and ground transportation is reimbursed at actual cost per TEP-006.
3. Daily rates
Per-diem rates by city tier (matching the lodging tier definitions in TEP-004 §3):
Tier Daily rate (USD)
Tier 1 $90
Tier 2 $70
Tier 3 $55
Tier 4 (international) Set at trip approval
4. Partial days
4.1. Departure day:  75% of the daily rate. 4.2. Return day:  75% of the daily rate. 4.3. Full
intermediate days:  100% of the daily rate.

5. Inclusions
The per-diem rate is intended to cover, in aggregate:
All three daily meals (breakfast, lunch, dinner) at typical mid-market quality
Non-alcoholic beverages
Reasonable incidentals (small purchases like bottled water, snacks, in-flight purchases under
$20)
Tips to housekeeping and other service staff
6. Exclusions
The per-diem does not cover:
Alcoholic beverages (which remain subject to TEP-003 — reimbursable only for sanctioned
client entertainment)
Client entertainment meals (which are reimbursed as itemized actual cost under TEP-002 §4,
not from per-diem)
Lodging or ground transportation
Phone, internet, or business communications charges
7. Receipts under per-diem
Individual meal receipts are not required when per-diem is elected (per TEP-007 §4.3). The employee
must, however, maintain documentation of trip duration and destination.
8. International travel
For international destinations not in Tier 1, Tier 2, or Tier 3, the per-diem rate is set at trip approval
based on the U.S. Department of State foreign per-diem rates (M&IE component) for the destination
city.
9. Definitions
Per diem  — A fixed daily allowance covering meals and incidentals, as an alternative to
itemized reimbursement.
M&IE  — Meals and Incidental Expenses, the component of U.S. federal per-diem rates that
covers meal-and-incidental costs (as distinct from lodging).
Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3 / Tier 4  — City classifications matching the Lodging Policy (TEP-004
§3).
Intermediate day  — A full day of business travel that is not the departure day or the return
day.•

Employee Grades Reference
Document:  TEP-009 Version:  1.2 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Human Resources, in
coordination with Finance Operations
1. Purpose
This reference document defines the Company's employee grade system as used in the Travel &
Expense Policy framework. It does not establish compensation or organizational authority — those
are governed by separate HR documents.
2. Grade ladder
Grade Typical title Approval authority
Grade 1Associate /
CoordinatorNone
Grade 2 Senior Associate None
Grade 3 Specialist Approves own travel up to $500
Grade 4 Senior Specialist Approves own travel up to $1,000
Grade 5 Manager Approves direct-report travel up to $2,000
Grade 6 Senior Manager Approves direct-report travel up to $3,000
Grade 7 Director Approves up to $5,000; first VP-level approval
Grade 8 Senior Director Approves up to $10,000

travel
Grade
10SVP / C-suite Unlimited within budget authority
3. Application to travel approvals
3.1. Per the Travel & Expense Policy (TEP-001 §4), submissions are subject to approval thresholds.
The grade ladder above defines who holds the relevant approval authority.
3.2. VP approval  (referred to throughout the policy suite) means approval from a Grade 9 employee
or above.

3.3. Director approval  means approval from a Grade 7 employee or above.
3.4. Manager approval  means approval from the employee's direct manager, regardless of that
manager's grade, provided the manager holds approval authority for the relevant amount.
4. Per-diem and lodging tier relationship
4.1. The employee's grade does not change the applicable per-diem rate or lodging tier cap. All
employees on the same trip are subject to the same caps.
4.2. Grade does affect approval thresholds (Section 3) but not entitlement to expense categories.
5. Acting capacity
Employees acting in a temporary higher-grade capacity (e.g., a Senior Manager covering for a
Director on leave) may exercise the approval authority of the higher grade only when formally
designated in writing by the absent manager's manager.
6. Definitions
Grade  — A numerical classification of an employee's level in the Company hierarchy. Grades
are independent of job family and title.
Approval authority  — The maximum dollar amount of expense the employee may approve for
others. An employee always retains full approval authority for their own expenses subject to
compliance.
Direct report  — An employee whose primary reporting line is to the approver, as reflected in
Workday.
Acting capacity  — A temporary formal designation of higher-grade authority during the
incumbent's absence.

Corporate Card Policy
Document:  TEP-010 Version:  2.2 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Finance Operations
1. Eligibility
1.1. Corporate Cards are issued to employees at Grade 4 and above, and to any employee whose
role requires regular business travel or client entertainment regardless of grade, subject to manager
nomination and Finance Operations approval.
1.2. Contractors and temporary employees are not eligible for Corporate Cards.
2. Use of the Corporate Card
2.1. The Corporate Card may be used only for business expenses that are reimbursable under the
Travel & Expense Policy framework.
2.2. Personal use of the Corporate Card is prohibited , even where the employee intends to repay
the Company.
2.3. The Corporate Card should be used as the primary payment method for business travel and
entertainment. Use of a personal card in lieu of the Corporate Card requires:
A documented business reason (e.g., the Corporate Card was declined, the vendor does not
accept the card brand)
Manager approval at the time of reimbursement
Submission of the personal card receipt with the expense report
3. Expense submission
3.1. All Corporate Card charges must be reconciled and submitted as part of an expense report within
30 days of the statement date, even if the charge is suspect or under dispute.
3.2. Unreconciled charges over 60 days old will be deducted from the employee's payroll without
further notice, in accordance with the Cardholder Agreement signed at card issuance.
4. Card security
4.1. The Corporate Card may not be shared, lent, or used by anyone other than the named
cardholder.• 
• 
•

4.2. Lost or stolen cards must be reported to the card issuer and to Finance Operations within 24
hours of discovery.
4.3. The card PIN may not be written down or stored alongside the card.
5. Prohibited charges
The following are explicitly prohibited on the Corporate Card and constitute a policy violation even if
subsequently disputed or refunded:
Cash advances (except where authorized for international travel)
Personal purchases of any kind
Gambling, adult entertainment, or political contributions
Alcohol in violation of TEP-003 (Alcohol Policy)
Purchases on behalf of family members, friends, or third parties
6. Disputed charges
6.1. Charges the cardholder believes to be fraudulent or in error must be disputed with the card issuer
within the issuer's dispute window (typically 60 days from the statement date).
6.2. Disputed charges still appear on the expense report. The cardholder should mark them as
"Disputed — Awaiting Resolution" and submit supporting documentation.
7. Card return
The Corporate Card must be returned to Finance Operations on the employee's last working day.
Charges incurred after the last working day are the employee's personal liability and will be
recovered.
8. Definitions
Corporate Card  — A commercial credit card issued in the employee's name under the
Company's commercial card program with the card issuer.
Cardholder Agreement  — The contract signed by the employee at card issuance establishing
personal liability for misuse.
Reconciliation  — The process of matching each card statement charge to a submitted
expense report line item with appropriate documentation.
Dispute  — A formal challenge to a card charge filed with the card issuer alleging fraud or error.

Gifts and Entertainment Policy
Document:  TEP-012 Version:  1.4 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Legal & Finance
Operations
1. Scope
This policy governs the giving and receiving of gifts, hospitality, and entertainment in the context of
business relationships with clients, vendors, prospects, and other external parties. It supplements the
Code of Conduct (COC-001) and the Meals and Entertainment Policy (TEP-002).
2. Guiding principles
2.1. Gifts and entertainment should be modest, infrequent, and clearly intended to maintain a
business relationship — not to influence a specific business decision.
2.2. When in doubt, decline.
3. Gifts received from third parties
3.1. Cash and cash equivalents  (including gift cards exceeding $25) may never be accepted,
regardless of source.
3.2. Non-cash gifts  may be accepted from a single source up to a value of $50 per gift and $200
cumulatively per calendar year .
3.3. Gifts valued above $50 must be declined and returned, with a polite note referencing this policy.
Where return is impractical (e.g., perishable items, hand-delivered ceremonial gifts in cultures where
refusal is offensive), the employee should accept the item and turn it over to HR for donation or
shared distribution.
3.4. Promotional items of nominal value (branded pens, notepads, bottles of water) are not
considered gifts under this policy.
4. Gifts given to clients and prospects
4.1. Client gifts are permitted up to $100 per recipient per calendar year , aggregated across all gifts
to that individual.
4.2. Gifts to government officials, public-sector employees, or representatives of state-owned
enterprises are prohibited unless specifically approved by the General Counsel in writing.

4.3. Cash and cash-equivalent gifts to clients are prohibited under all circumstances.
5. Entertainment
5.1. Client entertainment (meals, events, sporting events, etc.) is governed by the per-person caps in
the Meals and Entertainment Policy (TEP-002 §4).
5.2. Entertainment that includes spouses or family members of either party requires VP-level approval
and must have a clear business rationale.
5.3. Tickets to events  (concerts, sporting events, theater) provided to employees by a third party
must be declared if the face value exceeds $50. If an employee is invited to attend an event in the
company of the third party (i.e., the third party is also attending and there is a business discussion),
the event is treated as entertainment rather than a gift, and the value limits in 3.2 do not apply — but
the engagement should still be reasonable in frequency.
6. Anti-bribery
6.1. Nothing in this policy authorizes, condones, or permits any payment or thing of value provided
with the intent of influencing an official act, securing an improper advantage, or rewarding the
recipient for past favorable treatment. Such conduct violates the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, the
UK Bribery Act, and equivalent laws in other jurisdictions where the Company operates.
6.2. Suspected bribery should be reported immediately to the General Counsel or via the Ethics
Hotline.
7. Recording and reporting
7.1. Gifts and entertainment that fall within the policy limits do not require individual disclosure.
7.2. Gifts and entertainment received above the limits in Section 3 must be reported to the
employee's manager and to HR within 5 business days of receipt.
7.3. Business entertainment expensed to the Company is recorded on the expense report and is
subject to the Meals and Entertainment Policy (TEP-002).
8. Definitions
Gift — Anything of value transferred without commensurate consideration. Includes physical
items, services, cash, gift cards, tickets, and other tangible benefits.

8. Definitions
Gift — Anything of value transferred without commensurate consideration. Includes physical
items, services, cash, gift cards, tickets, and other tangible benefits.
Promotional item  — A branded item of nominal value (typically under $10) intended to
promote the giver, such as pens, notepads, or branded merchandise.
Nominal value  — Generally items under $10, distributed broadly rather than personally
targeted.• 
• 
•

Government official  — Any official, employee, or representative of any government agency,
state-owned enterprise, or public international organization, including political candidates and
party officials.

International Travel Policy
Document:  TEP-013 Version:  1.5 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Finance Operations &
Risk Management
1. Scope
This policy supplements the general Travel & Expense Policy framework with rules specific to
international business travel — defined as travel between countries, including travel between the
United States and Canada.
2. Approval
2.1. All international travel  requires advance written approval from a Vice President (Grade 9) or
above, in addition to the standard approval thresholds in TEP-001 §4.
2.2. Approval requests should be submitted at least 14 days prior to departure to allow time for travel
risk review, visa requirements, and currency arrangements.
3. Travel risk
3.1. International travel to destinations classified as high-risk by Northwind's travel risk vendor
(currently International SOS) requires additional security and Legal review.
3.2. Travel to countries under U.S. economic sanctions is prohibited absent an Office of Foreign
Assets Control (OFAC) license.
4. Lodging
4.1. International destinations not in Tier 1, Tier 2, or Tier 3 of the Lodging Policy (TEP-004 §3) fall
under Tier 4, where the rate cap is set at trip approval.
4.2. The approver should reference the U.S. Department of State's per-diem rates for the destination
as a benchmark.
5. Per-diem for international travel
5.1. International per-diem rates are set at the U.S. Department of State M&IE rate for the destination
city, at the time of trip approval.
5.2. International per-diem may be elected for trips of 3 or more nights, consistent with TEP-008.

6. Air travel
6.1. Business class  is permitted on international segments of 10 hours or more with VP approval, per
TEP-005 §2.3.
6.2. Premium economy is the default for international segments of 6 hours or more.
6.3. Stopovers added for personal reasons are at the employee's expense, with a comparison quote
required at booking.
7. Currency and foreign transaction fees
7.1. Currency conversion.  Expenses incurred in foreign currency should be reported in U.S. dollars
at the exchange rate on the date of the transaction, using the rate posted by the Corporate Card
issuer where applicable, or the OANDA daily rate otherwise.
7.2. Foreign transaction fees  charged by the Corporate Card issuer are reimbursable as a separate
line item, not bundled into the underlying expense.
7.3. Cash advances  for international travel are permitted in limited amounts and require advance
approval from Finance Operations.
8. Visas, immunizations, and passport fees
8.1. Visa fees, visa-application courier fees, and required immunizations for business travel are
reimbursable at actual cost.
8.2. Passport application or renewal fees are not reimbursable unless the employee is required to
obtain a passport solely for a specific business trip (e.g., previously did not need one and would not
need one for future personal use). Such cases are rare and require VP approval.
9. Alcohol
The Alcohol Policy (TEP-003) applies to international travel with the modification noted in TEP-003 §6:
where local law restricts alcohol, no alcohol is reimbursable regardless of business context.
10. Dependents
Travel costs for spouses, dependents, or other companions on international trips are never
reimbursable, per the general rule in TEP-005 §6.

11. Definitions
International travel  — Travel involving a border crossing, including U.S.-Canada and U.S.-
Mexico travel.
High-risk destination  — A country classified as elevated risk by the Company's contracted
travel risk vendor (currently International SOS); see SEC-301.
OFAC license  — Authorization issued by the U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control to engage in
otherwise-prohibited transactions in sanctioned jurisdictions.
Stopover  — A scheduled break in an itinerary, typically more than 24 hours, not necessary for
the routing itself.

Conference Attendance Policy
Document:  TEP-014 Version:  1.3 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Learning & Development,
in coordination with Finance Operations
1. Scope
This policy governs employee attendance at external conferences, training events, industry summits,
and similar professional development activities involving travel or significant Company expense.
2. Eligibility and approval
2.1. Conference attendance must be pre-approved in writing by the employee's manager. Approval
requests should include:
The conference name, dates, and location
Estimated total cost (registration + travel + lodging + meals)
Business justification — how the conference relates to the employee's role, team objectives, or
career development plan
Names of any other Northwind employees attending the same conference
2.2. Where the total cost exceeds $5,000, Director-level (Grade 7) approval is required. Where it
exceeds $10,000 or involves international travel, VP-level (Grade 9) approval is required, per TEP-001
§4.
3. Registration fees
3.1. Conference registration fees are reimbursable at the standard attendee rate.
3.2. Premium passes (VIP, all-access, executive tracks) are reimbursable only when they provide
demonstrable business value not available at the standard rate (e.g., access to specific workshops
critical to the employee's role).
3.3. Early-bird registration  should be elected whenever the conference offers it and the trip is
planned far enough in advance.
4. Travel and lodging
Travel and lodging to and from the conference are reimbursed under the general framework (TEP-004
Lodging, TEP-005 Air Travel, TEP-006 Ground Transportation).• 
• 
• 
•

5. Meals at conferences
5.1. Meals included with registration.  Where the conference registration fee includes meals (most
full-day conferences include lunch; many include breakfast and reception meals), no separate
reimbursement is available for those meals. The employee should consume the included meals.
5.2. Meals not included with registration  are reimbursed under the standard Meals and
Entertainment Policy (TEP-002) per-meal caps.
5.3. Off-site dinners with conference colleagues  are not automatically client entertainment. They
are treated as solo or team-only meals (TEP-002 §2) unless attended by an external client or
prospect.
6. Conference attendance with clients
6.1. Where an employee attends a conference together with a client (e.g., bringing a client to an
industry event), the related entertainment is governed by the Meals and Entertainment Policy
(TEP-002 §4) and may carry higher caps if VP approval is in place.
7. Extending trips
7.1. Employees may extend the trip for personal reasons (e.g., a weekend before or after the
conference). The Company pays only for the business portion of the trip.
7.2. Compare-quote requirement: where extending the trip increases the airfare, the employee must
provide a comparison quote at booking showing the cost for the business portion only, and the
difference is the employee's responsibility.
8. Conference deliverables
8.1. Employees attending an approved conference are expected to share key takeaways with their
team within 30 days of return. A short written summary (one page, or a presentation) is appropriate;
the format is at the manager's discretion.
8.2. Conference materials, slides, and recordings provided by the conference may be shared
internally consistent with the conference's attendee terms.
9. Non-attendance and cancellation
9.1. If an approved conference is cancelled or the employee can no longer attend, the employee
should pursue refunds of registration and travel costs to the extent practicable.
9.2. Non-refundable costs from a cancelled conference are absorbed by the Company provided the
cancellation was for a legitimate reason (manager decision, illness, business priority change).

10. Definitions
Conference  — A multi-session organized industry event involving travel or significant
Company expense, typically including paid registration.
Conference deliverables  — A short written summary or presentation prepared by the attendee
within 30 days of return.

Travel Risk and Security Protocol
Document:  SEC-301 Version:  1.4 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Risk Management 
Applies To:  All employees traveling internationally on Company business
1. Purpose
This protocol describes the risk assessment, preparation, and ongoing support that applies to
international business travel. Its purpose is to protect the safety, security, and well-being of traveling
employees and to enable the Company to fulfill its duty-of-care obligations.
2. Scope
This protocol applies to all international business travel — defined as travel involving a border
crossing in connection with Company business. It does not modify reimbursement rules, which are
governed by the International Travel Policy (TEP-013) and related travel & expense documents.
This protocol does not apply to domestic travel except where a specific destination has been
identified as elevated risk.
3. Travel risk vendor
3.1. The Company contracts with International SOS for travel risk intelligence and 24/7 medical and
security assistance. All international travelers are enrolled in International SOS automatically based on
travel bookings made through Concur Travel.
3.2. The International SOS Member Number for Company travelers is available in the Travel Portal.
3.3. International SOS contact: +1-215-942-8226 (24/7).
4. Pre-travel risk review
4.1. Country risk classification is published by International SOS. The current Company-recognized
tiers are:
Tier Description Action required
LowMost of Western Europe, USA/Canada, Australia, NZ,
Japan, SingaporeStandard travel booking
Medium Most of Latin America, Eastern Europe, much of AsiaStandard booking + traveler
briefing

Tier Description Action required
HighDesignated countries with elevated security, health, or
political riskVP approval + Risk Management
briefing
Extreme Active conflict zones, sanctioned countries Generally prohibited
4.2. Travel to High-tier destinations requires: - Vice President approval (Grade 9+) per TEP-013 §2 - A
pre-travel risk briefing with Risk Management - Confirmation of International SOS enrollment - An
itinerary submitted to Risk Management
4.3. Travel to Extreme-tier destinations is generally prohibited and requires CFO and General Counsel
approval, with a documented business necessity that could not be addressed remotely.
5. Visas, immunizations, and documentation
5.1. Travelers are responsible for obtaining required visas. Visa fees are reimbursable under TEP-013
§8.
5.2. Immunizations recommended for the destination should be obtained well in advance of travel.
The Company will reimburse for required immunizations.
5.3. A copy of the traveler's passport and itinerary should be left with a trusted contact at home.
6. In-country guidance
6.1. Communication.  Travelers should maintain regular communication with their home office and
family. International SOS may be used for routine status calls in elevated-risk destinations.
6.2. Lodging.  Stay in vetted lodging — preferably international hotel chains with consistent security
standards. Confirm the lodging is on the International SOS approved list for High-tier destinations.
6.3. Local transportation.  Vetted ground transportation should be arranged in advance for airport
transfers in High-tier destinations.
6.4. Documents.  Carry the passport, secondary photo ID, and a copy of the visa. Keep these secure
(hotel safe when not needed; document holder when needed).
6.5. Health.  Follow standard precautions for food and water in the destination. Pack a basic first-aid
kit. Identify the nearest International SOS-recommended medical facility.
7. Incidents
7.1. Medical emergency.  Call local emergency services and notify International SOS, who can
coordinate evacuation, second opinions, and home-office notification.

7.2. Security incident  (loss, theft, harassment, civil unrest). Move to safe location and call
International SOS for assistance.
7.3. Detention or arrest.  Identify yourself, do not resist, and request to contact the U.S. Embassy (or
relevant home-country embassy) and International SOS. Notify Risk Management as soon as
possible.
7.4. Death or serious injury.  Notify International SOS first, who will support family notification,
repatriation, and Company coordination.
8. Information security while traveling
8.1. Follow the Mobile Device Acceptable Use Standard (SEC-204) for handling devices
internationally, including the recommendation to use loaner devices in high-risk destinations.
8.2. Border searches of electronic devices are increasingly common. Travelers should assume that
devices may be inspected and should not carry sensitive data on the device when crossing borders.
8.3. Use VPN for all internet access while abroad.
9. Sanctions compliance
9.1. Travel to countries under U.S. economic sanctions (currently: Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Syria, and
certain regions of Ukraine and Russia) is prohibited absent an OFAC license. Travel approval requests
for these destinations are routed to General Counsel.
9.2. Sanctions screening is part of the pre-travel review for borderline destinations.
10. Personal travel combined with business
10.1. Combining personal travel with a business trip is acceptable. Reimbursement is only for the
business portion (TEP-005 §6.2, TEP-013 §6.3).
10.2. The risk and security protections in this document apply only to the business portion of the trip.
International SOS coverage during the personal portion is at the traveler's election; many home
insurance and credit card products provide travel medical coverage.
11. Returning travelers
11.1. After return from a High-tier destination, travelers should debrief with Risk Management within 5
business days. Debriefs help update the Company's risk understanding.
11.2. If symptoms of illness develop within 14 days of return from certain destinations (per CDC or
WHO advisories), notify HR before returning to the office; remote work may be advisable.

12. Definitions
High-tier destination  — A country categorized as elevated risk per International SOS.
OFAC  — U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control; administers economic sanctions programs.
International SOS  — The Company's contracted travel risk and medical assistance vendor.

Code of Conduct
Document:  COC-001 Version:  4.0 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Human Resources &
Legal
1. Purpose
This Code of Conduct sets out the ethical and professional standards expected of all Northwind
Logistics employees, contractors, and authorized representatives. Compliance is a condition of
employment.
2. Honesty and integrity
2.1. Employees are expected to be honest in all dealings with colleagues, clients, vendors, regulators,
and the public.
2.2. Misrepresentation of facts, falsification of records (including expense reports, time sheets, or
invoices), or concealment of material information is grounds for immediate termination.
3. Respect in the workplace
3.1. Northwind is committed to a workplace free from harassment, discrimination, and bullying.
Employees are expected to treat colleagues with respect regardless of race, ethnicity, gender, sexual
orientation, religion, age, disability, or any other protected characteristic.
3.2. Concerns or complaints should be raised with the employee's manager, HR, or via the
anonymous ethics hotline.
4. Conflicts of interest
4.1. Employees must disclose, in writing to their manager, any actual or potential conflict of interest.
Examples include:
Personal or family relationships with vendors, clients, or job candidates
Outside business interests that compete with the Company
Investments in Company clients or vendors above 1% ownership
4.2. Decisions made on behalf of the Company must be made in the Company's interest. Where a
conflict exists, the employee must recuse themselves from the relevant decision.• 
• 
•

5. Confidentiality
5.1. Employees have access to confidential information about the Company, its clients, its strategies,
and its employees. This information must not be disclosed outside the Company except as required
by the employee's role.
5.2. Confidentiality obligations survive the end of employment.
6. Use of company resources
6.1. Company resources (funds, equipment, time, intellectual property) are to be used for business
purposes. Limited and occasional personal use of equipment (e.g., personal email at lunch) is
acceptable; sustained personal use is not.
6.2. Expense reports submitted under the Travel & Expense Policy framework (TEP-001 et seq.) must
accurately reflect business expenses actually incurred. Inflated, fabricated, or duplicated expense
claims are treated as falsification of records under Section 2.
7. Gifts and entertainment
See the Gifts and Entertainment Policy (TEP-012) for specific rules. The principles in this section
apply:
7.1. Gifts and entertainment should never influence, or appear to influence, business decisions.
7.2. Employees should refuse gifts whose acceptance would create the appearance of impropriety,
regardless of monetary value.
8. Compliance with law
Employees must comply with all applicable laws and regulations in jurisdictions where the Company
operates, including but not limited to anti-bribery, anti-money-laundering, antitrust, employment, tax,
and securities laws.
9. Reporting violations
9.1. Employees who become aware of a potential violation of this Code are expected to report it
through one of the following channels:
Direct manager
Skip-level manager
Human Resources
General Counsel
Anonymous Ethics Hotline (operated by a third party)• 
• 
• 
• 
•

9.2. The Company prohibits retaliation against any employee who reports a concern in good faith,
even if the concern proves unfounded after investigation.
10. Enforcement
Violations of this Code are subject to disciplinary action up to and including termination. Certain
violations may also be reported to law enforcement or regulatory authorities.
11. Definitions
Conflict of interest  — A situation in which an employee's personal interests, relationships, or
outside activities could compromise — or appear to compromise — their judgment, objectivity,
or loyalty to the Company.
Confidential information  — Non-public information about the Company, its clients,
employees, vendors, or strategy, the disclosure of which could harm the Company or any of its
stakeholders.
Material violation  — A violation of this Code that has, or could have, a significant effect on the
Company, its operations, its reputation, or compliance with law.
Good faith  — Acting on a reasonable belief in the truth or significance of one's statements or
actions.

Remote Work Stipend Policy
Document:  HRP-015 Version:  1.1 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Human Resources
1. Scope
This policy governs Company support for remote and hybrid work arrangements, including one-time
home office setup and ongoing monthly stipends. It does not govern travel and is not part of the
Travel & Expense Policy framework.
2. Eligibility
2.1. Employees in roles formally designated as Remote  or Hybrid  are eligible for this stipend. Roles
designated as In-Office  are not eligible.
2.2. The role designation is recorded in the employee's HR record and may be changed only with the
agreement of the employee's manager and HR.
3. One-time home office setup
3.1. New remote and hybrid employees are eligible for a one-time home office setup allowance of 
$1,500  (USD), to be used within the first 90 days of employment in the eligible role.
3.2. The allowance may be used for:
Desk
Office chair
Monitor (single, up to 32 inches)
Keyboard and mouse
Webcam and microphone
Desk lamp
Cable management and small accessories
3.3. The following are not eligible under the one-time allowance:
Computers, tablets, and phones (provided separately by IT)
Office furniture beyond the desk-and-chair pairing (e.g., bookshelves, filing cabinets)
Decor, plants, or aesthetic items
Software and subscriptions
3.4. Receipts are required for all purchases under this allowance, per the standard receipt
requirements (TEP-007).• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
•

4. Monthly stipend
4.1. Eligible employees receive a monthly remote-work stipend of $80, intended to offset internet,
electricity, and basic supplies costs associated with working from home.
4.2. The monthly stipend is paid through payroll, is not a reimbursement, and does not require
receipts.
4.3. The stipend is treated as taxable income to the employee, consistent with applicable tax rules.
5. Return and recapture
5.1. Items purchased under the one-time setup allowance remain the employee's property and do not
need to be returned at end of employment, provided the employee has been in the eligible role for at
least 12 months.
5.2. Employees who separate from the Company within 12 months of receiving the setup allowance
are subject to pro-rata recapture of the allowance through final-payroll deduction.
6. Coordination with travel policy
6.1. The Remote Work Stipend is distinct from travel reimbursements. Travel between the employee's
home and a Company office is generally considered commuting (not reimbursable per TEP-006 §4.2),
unless the employee is designated Remote and the office visit is at the Company's specific request.
6.2. When a Remote employee is asked by the Company to travel to a Company office for a meeting,
training, or event, the trip is treated as business travel and is reimbursed under the standard
framework.
7. Definitions
Remote  — A role designation where the employee primarily works from a non-Company
location and is not expected to be in an office regularly.
Hybrid  — A role designation where the employee splits time between a Company office and a
non-Company location, typically with a minimum in-office cadence set by the manager.
In-Office  — A role designation where the employee is expected to be at a Company office
during normal working hours.
Setup allowance  — The one-time allowance for home office equipment described in §3.

Workplace Inclusion and Anti-Discrimination Policy
Document:  HR-104 Version:  3.0 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Human Resources Applies
To: All Northwind Logistics employees, contractors, applicants, vendors, and guests
1. Purpose
Northwind Logistics is committed to maintaining a workplace where every employee is treated with
dignity and respect, has equal access to opportunity, and can contribute their best work without
facing discrimination, harassment, or retaliation. This policy describes the standards of conduct
expected of every person on Company premises and the procedures available to those who
experience or witness violations.
2. Scope
This policy applies in every Company workplace — corporate offices, distribution centers, customer
sites, Company vehicles, and Company-sponsored events. It applies to in-person and remote
interactions, including communications via email, chat, video conference, and social media.
3. Equal opportunity
3.1. The Company prohibits discrimination against employees and applicants on the basis of race,
color, ethnicity, national origin, ancestry, religion, sex, gender, gender identity, gender expression,
sexual orientation, marital status, pregnancy, age, disability, genetic information, military or veteran
status, or any other classification protected by applicable law.
3.2. Hiring, compensation, promotion, training, discipline, and termination decisions are made on the
basis of legitimate, job-related factors.
3.3. The Company complies with all applicable laws regarding equal opportunity, including in the
United States: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act, the Americans with Disabilities Act, the Age
Discrimination in Employment Act, the Equal Pay Act, the Pregnancy Discrimination Act, and state
and local equivalents.
4. Harassment
4.1. Harassment is unwelcome conduct based on a protected characteristic that creates a hostile,
intimidating, or offensive work environment, or that affects employment decisions.
4.2. Examples include but are not limited to: slurs or jokes targeting a protected characteristic,
displaying offensive material, unwanted physical contact, sexual advances, propositions, or pressure
for personal interactions.

4.3. Sexual harassment is a specific form of harassment and is prohibited regardless of the genders
or relationship of the parties involved.
5. Bullying and uncivil conduct
5.1. Even where conduct does not rise to the level of unlawful harassment, the Company expects
civility. Bullying — repeated unreasonable conduct directed at an employee that intimidates,
humiliates, or undermines — is prohibited.
5.2. Disagreement, criticism, and performance feedback delivered respectfully are not bullying.
6. Accommodation
6.1. The Company provides reasonable accommodation to employees and applicants with
disabilities, sincerely held religious beliefs, pregnancy-related conditions, or other qualifying needs.
6.2. Accommodation requests should be directed to HR or through the Workday accommodation
portal.
7. Reporting
7.1. Employees who experience or witness conduct violating this policy are encouraged to report it
through any of: direct manager, skip-level manager, Human Resources, the General Counsel, or the
anonymous Ethics Hotline (operated by an independent third party).
7.2. Reports may be made anonymously. The Company treats reports confidentially to the extent
practical given the need to investigate.
8. Investigation
8.1. Reports are investigated promptly, typically within 30 days, by HR or a designated investigator
independent of the alleged conduct.
8.2. Investigators may interview involved parties, review relevant documents, and consult subject-
matter experts.
8.3. The Company communicates investigation outcomes to the reporter and the subject of the report
to the extent permitted by privacy considerations and applicable law.
9. Non-retaliation
9.1. The Company prohibits retaliation against any employee who, in good faith, reports a concern,
participates in an investigation, or refuses to participate in conduct that violates this policy.

9.2. Retaliation includes adverse employment action, exclusion, and informal mistreatment.
9.3. Retaliation is itself a violation of this policy and may result in disciplinary action up to termination.
10. Discipline
Violations of this policy result in disciplinary action proportionate to the severity, including written
warning, performance plan, demotion, or termination. The Company also reserves the right to pursue
civil or criminal remedies where warranted.
11. Training
11.1. All employees complete inclusion and anti-harassment training within 30 days of hire and
annually thereafter.
11.2. People managers complete additional manager-specific training covering reporting obligations,
accommodation processes, and difficult conversations.
12. Definitions
Protected characteristic  — A characteristic identified in Section 3.1 or under applicable law.
Adverse employment action  — A material change in the terms or conditions of employment,
including termination, demotion, denial of promotion, significant reduction in responsibilities, or
material change in compensation.
Good faith  — Acting on a reasonable belief in the truth of one's statements, regardless of
whether the statements ultimately prove correct.

Employee Referral Program
Document:  HR-208 Version:  1.3 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Talent Acquisition Applies
To: All Northwind Logistics employees in eligible roles
1. Purpose
The Employee Referral Program (ERP) recognizes that employees are often best positioned to identify
strong candidates for open Company roles. This document describes the program structure, referral
process, bonuses, and the conditions under which bonuses are paid.
2. Eligibility
2.1 Referrer eligibility
2.1.1. All full-time and part-time employees of Northwind Logistics in active status are eligible to refer
candidates, with the following exceptions:
Members of the Talent Acquisition team
Hiring managers for the specific role being filled
The candidate's potential direct manager
Members of the executive team (Grade 9+) — eligible to refer but not to receive a bonus
2.1.2. Contractors and temporary employees are not eligible to participate.
2.2 Candidate eligibility
2.2.1. To qualify for a referral bonus, the candidate must:
Not have applied to the same role independently in the prior 90 days
Not be currently employed by Northwind Logistics or its affiliates
Not have been employed by Northwind Logistics or its affiliates within the prior 12 months
(rehires are managed separately)
Be referred via the ERP portal (referrals via personal email or LinkedIn DM without portal
submission do not qualify)
3. Referral process
3.1. Submit a referral via the ERP portal at careers.northwindlogistics.com/referral, including the
candidate's name, email, the role of interest, and (optionally) the referrer's notes about candidate fit.• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
•

3.2. The candidate receives an invitation email to apply through the standard application flow, with the
referral attribution captured automatically.
3.3. Once the candidate applies, the standard recruiting process proceeds. The referrer may be asked
for additional context but does not participate in interviews or decisions.
4. Referral bonuses
4.1 Bonus amounts by role
Role band Bonus (USD, gross)
Individual contributor — Grades 1–4 $2,500
Individual contributor — Grades 5–6 $4,000
Manager / Senior IC — Grades 5–7 $5,000
Director — Grade 7+ $7,500
Vice President — Grade 9+ $12,500
Strategic / hard-to-fill roles (designated by recruiting) Up to $15,000
4.2 Bonus payment
4.2.1. Bonuses are paid in two installments:
50% on the candidate's start date
50% after the candidate has been employed for 90 calendar days in good standing
4.2.2. Bonuses are paid through payroll and are subject to applicable taxes.
4.3 Multiple referrers
If multiple employees independently refer the same candidate prior to the candidate's application, the
first attributed referral receives the bonus.
4.4 Bonus forfeiture
The bonus is forfeited if:
The referrer leaves the Company before either payment milestone
The candidate leaves the Company before the 90-day milestone (second installment only)
The referral is found to have been made in violation of this policy or other Company policies• 
• 
• 
• 
•

5. Diversity referral initiative
5.1. The Company periodically designates certain underrepresented-talent referrals for enhanced
bonuses, typically a 50% uplift above the standard amount.
5.2. Enhanced bonuses are not automatic; the recruiting team identifies eligible referrals based on
self-disclosed demographic information consistent with applicable law.
6. Conflicts of interest
6.1. Referrals of family members, household members, or close personal relations must be disclosed
in the referral submission. Whether to advance such referrals is at the recruiting team's discretion in
consultation with HR.
6.2. Referrals creating a potential reporting relationship (e.g., the referrer would become the
candidate's direct manager) generally are not eligible for bonus, even if the hire proceeds.
7. Tax treatment
Referral bonuses are taxable income to the referrer and are reported through payroll consistent with
applicable tax rules.
8. Privacy
Candidate information submitted via the ERP is handled consistent with the Candidate Privacy Notice
and is not disclosed to other employees beyond the recruiting and hiring team for the role.
9. Restrictions
9.1. Referrers may not represent themselves to candidates as having authority over the hiring
decision, salary, or other employment terms.
9.2. Pressuring candidates to apply, sharing confidential Company information with candidates pre-
employment, or making representations beyond publicly available role information is prohibited.
9.3. Use of the ERP to refer candidates for the purpose of generating referral bonuses without a
good-faith belief in candidate fit is prohibited and may result in disciplinary action.
10. Definitions
Referrer  — An eligible employee who submits a candidate via the ERP portal.
Active status  — Employed by the Company, not on a performance improvement plan that
explicitly suspends eligibility for incentive programs.• 
•

Designated strategic role  — A role flagged by Talent Acquisition leadership as eligible for an
enhanced bonus due to difficulty of fill or business priority.

Document:  REC-001 Version:  2.0 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  General Counsel Applies
To: All Northwind Logistics employees responsible for creating or maintaining business records
1. Purpose
This schedule defines how long the Company retains different categories of business records and the
manner of disposition at the end of the retention period. Adherence supports compliance with
applicable tax, audit, employment, and litigation-hold obligations, and reduces unnecessary storage
of stale or sensitive information.
2. Scope
The schedule covers all business records of the Company, regardless of medium (paper, electronic,
image, voice). It does not cover personal records of employees, transitory communications, or
duplicates of officially retained records.
3. Retention periods

destruction

destruction

destruction

destruction

Record Retention period
Personnel files (active employee) Duration of employment
Personnel files (former employee) 7 years post-termination
I-9 employment authorization 3 years from hire or 1 year from termination, whichever is later
Benefits enrollment 6 years
Workers' compensation claims 30 years (or per state law)
Performance reviews 3 years
Training completion records 5 years
3.3 Legal and regulatory records
Record Retention period
Executed contracts 7 years post-termination
Litigation and investigation files Per litigation hold; otherwise 7 years post-resolution
Regulatory filings Permanent
Insurance policies 10 years post-policy-period
Real-estate leases 10 years post-termination
3.4 Operational records
Record Retention period
Shipping records and freight invoices 7 years
Customer orders and order acknowledgments 7 years
Vendor records (active vendor) Duration of relationship
Vendor records (former vendor) 7 years post-offboarding
Quality / compliance audit reports 7 years

3.5 Information technology records
Record Retention period
System access logs 1 year
Security incident logs 3 years (longer if litigation-related)
Source code Duration of asset lifecycle + 3 years
Backups Per Backup Policy (IT-405)
4. Disposition
4.1. Secure destruction  means destruction in a manner that renders the record unreadable and
unrecoverable. For paper, this means cross-cut shredding or pulping; for electronic media, secure
wipe or physical destruction; for cloud-resident data, deletion through the platform's documented
data-deletion process plus verification.
4.2. Archive  means transfer to long-term off-site or cloud archival storage with limited access and
documented retention beyond the active-record period.
4.3. Records under a litigation hold are exempt from this schedule until the hold is lifted in writing by
Legal. The litigation-hold obligation overrides any retention period in this schedule.
5. Implementation
5.1. Department leaders are responsible for ensuring records under their stewardship are maintained
per this schedule.
5.2. Annual record review and disposition events should be conducted in Q1 of each year, covering
records that exceeded their retention period in the prior year.
5.3. Records Management staff are available to advise on classification and disposition questions and
may be reached at records@northwindlogistics.com.
6. Legal holds
6.1. When litigation, investigation, or regulatory inquiry is reasonably anticipated, Legal will issue a
written litigation hold notice to affected custodians. Records covered by the hold must be preserved
regardless of this schedule.
6.2. Custodians must acknowledge receipt of litigation hold notices within 5 business days and
confirm preservation measures.

Record  — Information created, received, or maintained as evidence of a transaction, decision,
or activity, in fulfillment of legal obligation or business need.
Transitory communications  — Drafts, working notes, and routine emails of no enduring
business value.
Litigation hold  — A formal directive from Legal suspending normal retention disposal for
specified records.
