Whistleblower and Ethics Reporting Policy
Document:  LEG-101 Version:  2.4 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  General Counsel Applies
To: All employees, contractors, vendors, and other persons reporting concerns regarding Northwind
Logistics
1. Purpose
The Company is committed to maintaining the highest standards of ethics, integrity, and legal
compliance. This policy establishes the channels through which concerns about suspected violations
may be raised and the protections available to those who raise them in good faith.
2. Scope
This policy covers concerns about suspected violations of law, regulation, the Company's Code of
Conduct, or other Company policies, including but not limited to: financial misconduct, fraud, bribery
and corruption, anti-trust violations, sanctions violations, environmental violations, harassment and
discrimination, retaliation, and conflicts of interest.
3. Reporting channels
Concerns may be raised through any of the following channels:
3.1. Direct manager.  Most concerns are best resolved through direct discussion with one's manager.
3.2. Skip-level manager.  Where direct discussion is not appropriate or has not produced a
satisfactory response, the manager's manager.
3.3. Human Resources.  For concerns involving people-related matters, including discrimination,
harassment, or workplace conduct.
3.4. General Counsel.  For concerns involving legal or regulatory matters, contract violations, or
fiduciary issues.
3.5. Internal Audit.  For concerns involving financial reporting, internal controls, or accounting
matters.
3.6. Anonymous Ethics Hotline.  A 24/7 hotline operated by an independent third-party vendor,
accessible by phone (1-800-NW-ETHIC) or by web (ethics.northwindlogistics.com). Anonymous
reporting is supported.

4. Anonymity and confidentiality
4.1. Reporters may elect to provide their name or to remain anonymous. The Company encourages
identified reporting because it permits more thorough investigation and follow-up, but anonymity is
fully supported.
4.2. The identity of reporters who provide their name is treated as confidential to the extent practical
given the need to investigate. Identity may be disclosed where required by law, by court order, or
where it becomes unavoidable in the course of investigation.
5. Good-faith standard
5.1. Reports made in good faith are protected by this policy regardless of whether the underlying
concern is ultimately substantiated.
5.2. Knowingly false reports made in bad faith are themselves a violation of this policy and may result
in disciplinary action.
6. Anti-retaliation
6.1. The Company strictly prohibits retaliation against any person who, in good faith, makes a report
under this policy, participates in an investigation, or refuses to participate in conduct that they
reasonably believe violates law, regulation, or Company policy.
6.2. Retaliation includes — but is not limited to — termination, demotion, denial of promotion,
material change in working conditions, exclusion from meetings or projects, and informal
mistreatment.
6.3. Retaliation may itself be reported under this policy. Reports of retaliation are investigated with the
same seriousness as the underlying concern.
7. Investigation
7.1. Reports are reviewed by the Ethics Office (within the Office of the General Counsel) within 5
business days of receipt and assigned to an appropriate investigator.
7.2. Investigations are conducted by individuals independent of the alleged conduct and trained in
investigation methodology.
7.3. Investigations may involve interviewing the reporter (where identified), the subject of the report,
witnesses, and other relevant parties; reviewing documents and records; and consulting subject-
matter experts.
7.4. Investigations are completed as promptly as the facts permit. Typical timelines are 30–60 days;
complex matters may take longer.

7.5. To the extent permitted by privacy considerations and applicable law, the reporter (where
identified) and the subject are informed of the investigation outcome.
8. Whistleblower legal protections
8.1. In the United States, multiple statutes provide additional legal protections for whistleblowers,
including the Sarbanes-Oxley Act, the Dodd-Frank Act, the False Claims Act, and various sector-
specific laws.
8.2. Nothing in this policy or any Company agreement is intended to limit a reporter's right to
communicate concerns to government regulators, including the SEC, OSHA, the Department of
Labor, the DOJ, or equivalent state and international authorities. Such communications may be made
without prior Company notice or consent.
8.3. Awards or bounties available under whistleblower laws (e.g., SEC whistleblower program) are not
affected by this policy.
9. Roles
9.1. The General Counsel oversees this policy.
9.2. The Audit Committee of the Board of Directors receives a quarterly summary of reports and
investigations, with detail proportionate to the matter's significance.
9.3. The Company contracts with a third-party ethics hotline provider; the contract requires the
provider to maintain the confidentiality and anonymity options of the channel.
10. Definitions
Good faith  — Acting on a reasonable belief in the truth or significance of one's statements,
regardless of whether they prove correct.
Retaliation  — Adverse action taken against a person because of their good-faith engagement
with this policy.
Ethics Office  — A function within the Office of the General Counsel responsible for receiving
and triaging reports under this policy.

Intellectual Property and Proprietary Information Policy
Document:  LEG-203 Version:  2.2 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  General Counsel Applies
To: All Northwind Logistics employees, contractors, and authorized agents
1. Purpose
This policy describes the Company's ownership of intellectual property developed in the course of
employment, the protection of proprietary information, the use of third-party intellectual property, and
the obligations of employees in connection with the Company's IP and that of others.
2. Scope
The policy applies to all forms of intellectual property — copyrights, patents, trademarks, trade
secrets, and confidential know-how — and to all employees, contractors, and authorized agents of
the Company globally.
3. Ownership of IP developed during employment
3.1. Work made for hire.  Subject to applicable law, all intellectual property created by an employee in
the course of employment, using Company resources, or related to the Company's business, is the
property of the Company.
3.2. Assignment.  Where the doctrine of "work made for hire" does not assign IP automatically,
employees assign such IP to the Company by their employment agreement and acknowledgement of
this policy.
3.3. Exclusions.  IP developed by an employee entirely on their own time, without Company
resources, and unrelated to the Company's business or anticipated business, is generally the
employee's. Employees in California, Washington, and certain other states have specific statutory
protections; the employment agreement reflects these.
4. Inventions
4.1. Inventions made during employment are disclosed to the Company via the Invention Disclosure
form in the Self-Service Portal.
4.2. The Company's Patent Committee reviews invention disclosures and decides on patent
prosecution.
4.3. Inventors named on issued Company patents receive a patent issuance award per the Inventor
Recognition Program.

5. Open-source software
5.1. The Company permits and encourages thoughtful use of open-source software in Company
products and operations, subject to the Open Source Software Standard (LEG-204).
5.2. Use of open-source software in a Company product requires license review by Legal prior to
inclusion.
5.3. Strong copyleft licenses (e.g., GPLv3, AGPL) require additional review and are generally restricted
to internal-only use.
5.4. Employee contributions to open-source projects require pre-approval from the employee's
manager and Legal, particularly where the contribution involves code developed during employment
or relating to the Company's business.
6. Trademarks and branding
6.1. Use of Company names, logos, and trademarks must follow the Brand Guidelines (BRAND-001).
6.2. External communications using Company branding (presentations, customer-facing documents,
conference materials) should be reviewed for brand compliance.
6.3. Adoption of new product names or product trademarks requires Legal clearance to avoid
infringing third-party rights.
7. Trade secrets and proprietary information
7.1. The Company's trade secrets include — but are not limited to — pricing strategy, customer lists,
vendor terms, source code, proprietary algorithms, operational know-how, and unannounced product
or facility plans.
7.2. Employees must not disclose, use, or take Company trade secrets outside the scope of their
work, whether during employment or after.
7.3. Proprietary information remains the Company's property at the end of employment. Departing
employees must return all materials and confirm that they have not retained Company information
(including by employee certification).
8. Use of third-party IP
8.1. Use of third-party intellectual property in Company products or operations requires a valid
license, public-domain status, or fair-use justification reviewed by Legal.
8.2. Stock images, fonts, music, and similar assets used in Company materials must come from
properly licensed sources. Internal stock libraries are available via the brand portal.

8.3. Use of customer information, vendor information, or other third-party information held in
confidence is constrained by the relevant agreement; misuse may breach both contract and this
policy.
9. Generative AI
9.1. Outputs of generative AI tools may incorporate or be derived from third-party IP. Care must be
taken to verify the licensing implications of using AI-generated content in Company products.
9.2. Inputs to public AI tools should not include Company Confidential or Restricted data (see
SEC-201 and SEC-202).
9.3. The Generative AI Use Standard (LEG-205) provides detailed guidance.
10. Reporting
10.1. Suspected unauthorized use of Company IP by third parties should be reported to
ip@northwindlogistics.com.
10.2. Notices alleging that the Company is infringing third-party IP should be forwarded immediately
to Legal without response by the receiving employee.
11. Departing employees
11.1. Departing employees are reminded of their continuing confidentiality and assignment
obligations.
11.2. Employees going to work for a competitor or to start a competing business have specific
restrictions in their employment agreement (where enforceable under applicable law) and continuing
obligations regardless.
11.3. Exit interviews address these obligations explicitly.
12. Definitions
Work made for hire  — Under U.S. copyright law, a work prepared by an employee within the
scope of employment, with the employer as legal author.
Trade secret  — Information that derives independent economic value from not being generally
known and is the subject of reasonable measures to maintain secrecy.
Copyleft  — A licensing model requiring derivative works to be made available under the same
license, restricting proprietary use.•

Data Classification and Handling Standard
Document:  SEC-201 Version:  2.3 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Information Security 
Applies To:  All Northwind Logistics employees, contractors, and systems
1. Purpose
This standard defines how the Company classifies data assets by sensitivity and the controls required
for each classification. Adherence is mandatory and is a precondition for handling Company data.
2. Scope
The standard covers all data created, received, processed, transmitted, or stored on behalf of the
Company, in any medium — electronic, paper, image, voice, or other — across any platform, device,
or environment.
3. Classification levels
3.1 Public
Data approved for public release. Disclosure carries no risk to the Company. Examples: published
press releases, marketing collateral, regulatory filings already public, the Company website.
3.2 Internal
Data intended for internal use whose unauthorized disclosure would cause limited harm. Examples:
internal organizational charts, project plans, the employee directory, non-financial internal
communications, internal training materials.
3.3 Confidential
Data whose unauthorized disclosure could cause material harm to the Company, its customers, or its
employees. Examples: customer lists, contracts, pricing strategy, unannounced product or facility
decisions, personnel files, employee compensation, internal financial reports, audit findings.
3.4 Restricted
Data whose unauthorized disclosure could cause severe harm or significant regulatory liability.
Examples: payment card data (PAN), Social Security numbers, government-issued ID numbers,
source code for proprietary systems, M&A transaction data, customer data subject to specific
contractual protections.

4. Required controls by classification
4.1 Access
Classification Access principle
Public No restriction
Internal All employees by default; contractors with NDA
Confidential Need-to-know basis; access reviewed quarterly
Restricted Named individuals only; access reviewed monthly; multi-party approval to grant
4.2 Storage
Classification Storage requirement
Public Any
Internal Approved corporate platforms
Confidential Approved corporate platforms with audit logging
Restricted Approved corporate platforms with audit logging and encryption at rest
4.3 Transmission
Classification Transmission requirement
Public Any
Internal Encrypted in transit (TLS or equivalent)
Confidential Encrypted in transit; no transmission to personal email or unmanaged endpoints
Restricted Encrypted in transit and end-to-end; transmission requires DLP exception ticket
4.4 Retention
Retention requirements are governed by the Records Retention Schedule (REC-001). Where the
retention schedule and this standard conflict, the schedule controls.
5. Handling rules
5.1. Labeling.  Documents at Confidential or Restricted classification should be labeled at the top of
each page and in the document properties.

5.2. Sharing externally.  External sharing of Confidential data requires a signed NDA. External sharing
of Restricted data requires Legal and Security approval.
5.3. Printing.  Restricted data should not be printed except where required by law or regulator
request, in which case the printed copy must be retrieved immediately from the printer and securely
destroyed when no longer needed.
5.4. Mobile devices.  Confidential and Restricted data on mobile devices requires Mobile Device
Management (MDM) enrollment.
5.5. AI and analytics tools.  Confidential and Restricted data may not be submitted to public LLM
tools (ChatGPT, public Claude, etc.) or external analytics platforms not on the Approved Tools list.
6. Incident reporting
Suspected loss, theft, exposure, or unauthorized access of Confidential or Restricted data must be
reported to the Security team within 4 hours of discovery, via security@northwindlogistics.com or the
on-call security number.
7. Exceptions
Exceptions to specific controls in this standard require approval by the Chief Information Security
Officer, documented in writing, and reviewed at least annually.
8. Definitions
Data subject  — A natural person to whom personal data relates.
Audit logging  — System logging that records who accessed what data, when, and from
where.
Encryption at rest  — Encryption of stored data such that ciphertext is unintelligible without
keys held separately from the storage.
DLP — Data Loss Prevention, a class of controls that monitors and blocks unauthorized data
movement.

Information Technology Acceptable Use Policy
Document:  SEC-202 Version:  3.1 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Information Security 
Applies To:  All employees, contractors, and authorized third parties using Company IT resources
1. Purpose
This policy establishes acceptable use of the Company's information technology resources and the
conduct expected of users. Adherence is a condition of access.
2. Scope
The policy applies to all Company-owned, leased, or operated information technology resources,
including: computers, mobile devices, accounts, networks (wired, wireless, VPN), email and
messaging systems, file storage, cloud services, and any service accessed using Company
credentials.
It applies whether the user is on Company premises, at home, traveling, or elsewhere.
3. General use principles
3.1. Business purpose.  Company IT resources are provided for business purposes. Limited and
occasional personal use that does not interfere with work, consume material resources, or violate
other policies is acceptable.
3.2. No expectation of privacy.  The Company reserves the right to monitor, review, and audit use of
its IT resources, including content of files, communications, and browsing history, as permitted by
applicable law.
3.3. No transfer.  User accounts may not be shared. Users are responsible for activity occurring under
their accounts.
4. Authentication and access
4.1. All Company accounts must be protected by passwords meeting current strength requirements
(minimum 14 characters; complexity per the Password Standard SEC-203).
4.2. Multi-factor authentication is required for all Company accounts where supported. Disabling MFA
is prohibited.
4.3. Credentials must not be stored in plaintext, written down in unsecured locations, or shared via
unsecured channels.

5. Devices
5.1. Company-issued devices must enroll in Mobile Device Management (MDM) and remain enrolled
throughout their use.
5.2. Personal devices used to access Company resources must enroll in MDM for the Company
partition and meet baseline security configuration (OS patches current, full-disk encryption enabled,
screen lock enabled).
5.3. Devices must be locked when unattended and powered off or locked when traveling
internationally.
6. Networks
6.1. The Company-managed Wi-Fi network is for Company devices and registered personal devices.
Guest Wi-Fi is for visitors and personal use.
6.2. VPN must be used when accessing internal Company systems from outside the corporate
network, except for systems explicitly designated as accessible from the public internet.
6.3. Connection to untrusted networks (public Wi-Fi, hotel Wi-Fi, airport Wi-Fi) is permitted only via
VPN.
7. Email and messaging
7.1. Company email is for business communications. Personal email accounts may not be used for
Company business.
7.2. Phishing and impersonation attempts should be reported via the "Report Phish" button in the
email client or to security@northwindlogistics.com.
7.3. Sensitive information (Confidential or Restricted per SEC-201) should not be sent in unencrypted
email outside the Company.
8. Software
8.1. Only approved software may be installed on Company devices. The Approved Software list is
maintained in the Self-Service Portal.
8.2. Installation of software requires either pre-approval (Approved Software list) or a Software
Request Ticket.
8.3. Open-source components used in Company products are subject to the Open Source Policy
(LEG-203).

9. Prohibited activities
The following are prohibited:
9.1. Use of Company IT resources to violate any law or other Company policy.
9.2. Unauthorized access to systems, accounts, or data.
9.3. Circumventing or attempting to circumvent security controls.
9.4. Installation or use of cryptocurrency mining software on Company resources.
9.5. Use of Company resources for personal commercial activity unrelated to the Company.
9.6. Connecting unauthorized devices to Company networks (e.g., personal routers, IoT devices).
9.7. Sharing accounts or credentials, including with other Company employees.
10. Incident reporting
10.1. Suspected security incidents must be reported to the Security team within 4 hours of discovery.
10.2. Lost or stolen devices must be reported immediately so that remote wipe can be triggered.
11. Consequences
Violation of this policy may result in revocation of access, disciplinary action up to and including
termination, and civil or criminal liability where applicable.
12. Definitions
Approved Software  — Software listed in the Self-Service Portal as preinstalled or available for
self-install without ticket.
MDM  — Mobile Device Management; a system enabling the Company to enforce configuration
policy on enrolled devices.
VPN — Virtual Private Network; an encrypted connection between a remote device and the
corporate network.
MFA  — Multi-Factor Authentication.

Office Safety and Emergency Procedures
Document:  FAC-005 Version:  1.9 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Facilities & Environmental
Health and Safety (EHS) Applies To:  All employees, contractors, and visitors at Company offices
1. Purpose
This document describes the safety practices, emergency procedures, and reporting expectations for
all Company office facilities. The goal is a workplace that is safe by design and prepared for the range
of foreseeable emergencies.
2. Scope
The procedures apply at all Company office locations. Distribution centers and operations facilities
are covered by separate site-specific procedures supplementing this document.
3. Building access
3.1. All employees are issued an access badge upon hire. Badges must be visible while on premises
and surrendered at termination.
3.2. Tailgating (allowing another person to enter behind you without their own badge) is prohibited.
3.3. Visitors must be sponsored by an employee, sign in at reception, and wear a visitor badge.
4. Fire safety
4.1. Each floor has clearly posted evacuation routes and assembly points. Familiarize yourself with
the nearest two exits from your workstation.
4.2. Fire alarms  require immediate evacuation. Do not return to the building until cleared by Facilities
or the fire department.
4.3. Fire drills  are conducted twice annually at each location. Participation is mandatory.
4.4. Fire extinguishers are located at posted intervals. Use only if trained and only when evacuation is
in progress and the fire is small.
5. Medical emergencies
5.1. Call 911 (or local equivalent) immediately for serious medical emergencies.

5.2. Notify Reception so they can direct emergency responders to the location.
5.3. AEDs (automated external defibrillators) are located at each floor's reception area and labeled.
CPR-trained employees are listed on the floor's emergency contact poster.
5.4. First-aid kits are located at each floor's kitchen and reception.
6. Severe weather
6.1. Tornado / severe wind warnings  (where applicable): proceed to designated shelter areas —
interior corridors and stairwells away from exterior windows.
6.2. Earthquake  (where applicable): Drop, Cover, Hold On. Stay where you are during shaking;
evacuate only when shaking stops.
6.3. Severe winter weather  affecting commute is announced via Workplace messaging by 6 AM
local time. Office may be designated remote-only for the day.
7. Workplace injuries
7.1. All workplace injuries — no matter how minor — must be reported to EHS within 24 hours via the
Workplace Incident form in the Self-Service Portal.
7.2. Work-related injuries are covered by the Company's workers' compensation insurance. The form
intake triggers the claim process.
7.3. Manager assistance is available; HR and EHS coordinate workplace accommodation and return-
to-work planning.
8. Ergonomics
8.1. Sit-stand desks are available at each workstation. Chairs are adjustable.
8.2. Employees experiencing repetitive strain or other ergonomic discomfort may request an
ergonomic assessment via EHS.
8.3. Approved ergonomic accessories (lumbar support, keyboard tray, vertical mouse) are available
through the Self-Service Portal.
9. Hazard reporting
9.1. Observed hazards — spills, broken equipment, blocked exits, suspicious activity — should be
reported to Facilities at facilities@northwindlogistics.com or via the Facilities Hotline.

9.2. Imminent hazards should be addressed by securing the area (cordoning off, posting a warning
sign) and reporting immediately.
10. Hazardous materials
The Company does not maintain quantities of hazardous chemicals in office facilities beyond
standard cleaning supplies. Where applicable, Safety Data Sheets are maintained at Reception and
accessible to any employee on request.
11. Active threat
11.1. The Company subscribes to "Run, Hide, Fight" guidance for active threat situations. Familiarize
yourself with the training module in the Learning portal.
11.2. Active threat alerts will be communicated via the emergency notification system (text +
Workplace).
11.3. Do not attempt to coordinate or investigate. Follow instructions from responders.
12. Definitions
Assembly point  — A designated location outside the building where employees gather after
evacuation for headcount.
Tailgating  — Following another person through an access-controlled entry without using one's
own badge.
AED — Automated External Defibrillator.

Mobile Device Acceptable Use Standard
Document:  SEC-204 Version:  2.1 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Information Security 
Applies To:  All mobile devices used to access Northwind Logistics resources
1. Purpose
This standard establishes the configuration, use, and security expectations for mobile devices —
Company-issued and personally-owned — used to access Company resources. It supplements the
general Acceptable Use Policy (SEC-202) with mobile-specific requirements.
2. Scope
The standard applies to smartphones, tablets, and other portable computing devices used to access
Company email, files, applications, networks, or data, regardless of whether the device is owned by
the Company or by the employee.
3. Device categories
3.1 Company-issued devices
3.1.1. Devices purchased or leased by the Company and assigned to a specific employee.
3.1.2. Fully managed via Mobile Device Management (MDM). The Company has full configuration
authority over these devices.
3.1.3. Permitted models: latest two generations of iPhone (Apple); latest two generations of Samsung
Galaxy and Google Pixel (Android). Other models require an exception.
3.2 Personally-owned devices (BYOD)
3.2.1. Personal devices used to access Company resources must enroll the Company partition in
MDM.
3.2.2. The Company's MDM enforces configuration on the Company partition only. Personal apps and
data on the device remain under the employee's control.
3.2.3. Personal devices used for BYOD must meet baseline requirements: - Current vendor-supported
OS version - Manufacturer security patches current within 60 days - Screen lock with PIN, password,
or biometric - Full-device encryption enabled - Compatible with the Company's MDM platform
3.2.4. Jailbroken or rooted devices may not access Company resources.

4. Required configuration
4.1. Screen lock with at least 6-digit PIN, alphanumeric password, or biometric authentication; auto-
lock after 5 minutes of inactivity.
4.2. Device encryption enabled.
4.3. MDM enrolled and reporting compliant status.
4.4. Backup of Company data to a personal cloud (iCloud Backup, Google Drive Backup, etc.) is
disabled or scoped to exclude Company apps.
4.5. Find My Device or equivalent enabled, to support remote location and wipe in the event of loss.
5. Permitted apps
5.1. Approved corporate apps (email, calendar, document collaboration, chat, VPN) are installed via
the corporate app catalog.
5.2. Other apps installed on the device must not access Company data or credentials. Apps with
overly broad permissions (e.g., file-system-wide access) are flagged by MDM.
5.3. Public AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, public Claude, Gemini consumer apps) may be installed for
personal use but may not be used to process Company Confidential or Restricted data (see
SEC-201).
6. Travel with mobile devices
6.1. Domestic travel.  Devices should be locked when unattended.
6.2. International travel.  Devices crossing borders may be subject to inspection. Employees
traveling to high-risk destinations (refer to Travel Risk listings) may be issued loaner travel devices
upon request; these are wiped on return.
6.3. Powering off.  Devices crossing international borders should be powered off (not just locked)
prior to inspection.
7. Loss, theft, and remote wipe
7.1. Lost or stolen devices must be reported to security@northwindlogistics.com or by phone
immediately.
7.2. The Company's MDM enables remote wipe of the Company partition. For Company-issued
devices, full-device wipe may be triggered.

7.3. Personal devices that fail compliance checks (e.g., OS out of support, rooted) may have their
Company partition automatically wiped without further notice. Personal apps and data are preserved.
8. Employee departure
8.1. On departure, the Company's MDM removes the Company partition from personal devices.
8.2. Company-issued devices must be returned by the last working day.
9. Reimbursement
9.1. The Company does not reimburse employees for personal mobile device hardware or carrier
plans, except where a Company-issued device cannot meet a job requirement.
9.2. International roaming charges incurred during business travel are reimbursable per the Travel &
Expense Policy framework.
10. Privacy
10.1. The Company's MDM platform reports device compliance state, OS version, MDM-managed
app inventory, and approximate location of Company-issued devices.
10.2. On personal devices, the MDM platform does NOT report personal app inventory, photos,
messages, browsing history, or contacts.
10.3. The Privacy Notice for MDM (PRIV-105) describes the data collected by the MDM platform in
detail.
11. Definitions
MDM  — Mobile Device Management; a platform enabling configuration, monitoring, and
remote actions on enrolled devices.
BYOD  — Bring Your Own Device.
Company partition  — A separated, MDM-controlled set of apps and data on a personal
device.
Jailbreak / root  — Modification of a device's operating system to remove vendor-imposed
restrictions, weakening device security.

Sustainability and Environmental Responsibility Policy
Document:  SUS-001 Version:  2.1 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Office of Sustainability 
Applies To:  All Northwind Logistics employees and operating facilities
1. Purpose
Northwind Logistics is committed to operating in a manner that minimizes environmental impact,
supports the communities in which we operate, and contributes to the broader transition to a low-
carbon economy. This policy articulates our environmental commitments and the operational
expectations they create.
2. Scope
This policy applies to all Company-owned and Company-operated facilities, all Company vehicles
and fleet operations, all employee business activities, and all Company procurement decisions where
environmental considerations are material.
3. Carbon and energy
3.1. The Company has committed to a 50% reduction in Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by 2030,
against a 2019 baseline, and net-zero by 2045. Progress is reported annually in the Sustainability
Report.
3.2. Each facility manager is responsible for tracking and reporting monthly energy consumption
(electricity, natural gas, fuel) through the SustainTrack platform.
3.3. New equipment purchases over $5,000 must include an energy-efficiency comparison and,
where commercially reasonable, the more efficient option should be selected.
4. Waste and recycling
4.1. All offices will provide recycling collection for paper, cardboard, glass, aluminum, and plastics #1
and #2.
4.2. E-waste must be routed through the Company's approved e-waste recycler. Disposal of e-waste
in general trash is prohibited.
4.3. Single-use plastics (water bottles, utensils, plates) should be avoided in catered meetings and
Company events. Bulk-water dispensers and reusable serviceware are preferred.

5. Travel and commuting
5.1. Employees should consider the carbon impact of business travel when selecting itineraries.
Direct flights, ground transportation, and video meetings should be considered where the business
case permits.
5.2. Personal vehicle use for business is subject to the Ground Transportation Policy (TEP-006); this
policy does not modify those rules.
5.3. The Company offers pre-tax commuter benefits for public transit and a workplace EV charging
stipend at sites where chargers are installed.
6. Procurement
6.1. Procurement teams should consider environmental factors (vendor sustainability disclosures,
packaging waste, transportation distance) alongside cost and quality.
6.2. Office supply orders should default to recycled-content paper and refillable products where
available.
7. Reporting and metrics
7.1. The Office of Sustainability publishes quarterly internal dashboards covering energy
consumption, waste diverted from landfill, business-travel emissions, and water usage.
7.2. Material environmental incidents (spills, regulatory notices, complaints) must be reported to the
Office of Sustainability within 24 hours of discovery.
8. Training
8.1. New hires receive sustainability awareness training as part of onboarding.
8.2. Facility managers receive annual refresher training on environmental compliance, energy
reporting, and waste handling.
9. Governance
9.1. The Office of Sustainability reports to the Chief Operating Officer.
9.2. An employee Sustainability Council, with representation from each business unit, advises on
programs and reviews progress quarterly.

Vendor Onboarding and Third-Party Risk Standard
Document:  PROC-002 Version:  1.7 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Procurement & Vendor
Management Applies To:  All Northwind Logistics business units engaging third parties
1. Purpose
This standard establishes the process by which new vendors and third parties are onboarded to do
business with the Company, the risk reviews required prior to onboarding, and the ongoing oversight
expectations for active vendor relationships.
2. Scope
The standard applies to any third party paid or otherwise compensated by the Company in exchange
for goods, services, software, data, or rights, including contractors, consultants, software-as-a-
service providers, distributors, and resellers.
The standard does not apply to: employee expense reimbursements (governed by TEP-001),
customer-facing transactions, charitable donations, or government filings.
3. Onboarding workflow
3.1 Initiation
A business sponsor submits a Vendor Onboarding Request in the Procurement Portal, including:
vendor name, services or goods sought, estimated annual spend, data access required, and
proposed term.
3.2 Risk classification
Vendors are classified as Tier A, B, or C based on annual spend, data access, and operational
criticality. The classification drives the depth of due diligence.
Tier CriteriaDue diligence
depth
ASpend > $500k/year OR access to Confidential/Restricted data OR
critical operational dependencyFull review
B Spend $50k–$500k/year OR access to Internal data Standard review
C Spend < $50k/year AND no data access AND non-critical Light review

3.3 Due diligence
Standard and full reviews include vendor questionnaire, financial review (Bradstreet or equivalent),
security questionnaire (SIG-Lite or equivalent), insurance certificate review, and watchlist screening
(OFAC, EU sanctions, PEPs).
Full reviews additionally include on-site or virtual site visit, SOC 2 Type II review (or equivalent), and
reference checks with at least two existing customers of similar profile.
3.4 Contract
All vendor engagements require a written agreement. Standard agreements include the Company's
standard terms; deviations require Legal review.
3.5 Onboarding
Approved vendors are added to the vendor master with a unique vendor ID, banking details, and
payment terms (default Net 30, calendar days).
4. Ongoing oversight
4.1. Tier A vendors are reviewed annually; Tier B every 18 months; Tier C every 3 years or on material
change.
4.2. Material changes (ownership, location, business model, security posture) trigger an out-of-cycle
review.
4.3. Annual security questionnaire renewal is required for any vendor with Internal-or-above data
access.
5. Payment
5.1. Payment to vendors is processed by Accounts Payable based on approved invoices matched to
purchase orders.
5.2. Invoices missing a valid PO are routed to the business sponsor for confirmation before payment.
5.3. Wire instructions changes from a vendor require independent verbal verification with a known
contact at the vendor before any payment is sent to the new account. This is a fraud control and may
not be bypassed under time pressure.

6. Offboarding
6.1. When a vendor relationship ends, the business sponsor initiates an Offboarding Request,
including final invoice cutoff, data return or destruction requirements, system access removal, and
confirmation that all deliverables have been received.
6.2. Vendors with Confidential or Restricted data access must provide a Certificate of Destruction
within 30 days of offboarding.
7. Approved-vendor list
7.1. The Approved Vendor List, maintained in the Procurement Portal, identifies preferred vendors for
common categories. Use of an Approved Vendor satisfies the onboarding requirement for Tier B and
C engagements.
7.2. Adding a vendor to the Approved Vendor List requires CFO sign-off following full due diligence.
8. Exceptions
Exceptions to this standard require written approval from the Chief Procurement Officer. Emergency
engagements (operational continuity, security incident response) may proceed under expedited
review with retrospective documentation within 10 business days.
9. Definitions
Vendor  — A third party providing goods or services to the Company under a contract or PO.
Purchase order (PO)  — A document authorizing a vendor to deliver goods or services and
committing the Company to a defined payment.
Bradstreet  — Dun & Bradstreet credit and risk reporting service.
OFAC  — U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control sanctions list.

Charitable Giving and Volunteering Policy
Document:  HR-302 Version:  1.5 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  People Experience (HR) 
Applies To:  All Northwind Logistics employees
1. Purpose
The Company believes its long-term success is connected to the well-being of the communities in
which it operates. This policy describes the programs through which the Company and its employees
contribute to charitable causes — financial donations, matching gifts, volunteer time, and pro-bono
service.
2. Scope
This policy covers Company-sponsored charitable activity and employee participation programs. It
does not cover personal charitable activity unrelated to the Company.
3. Programs available
3.1 Matching gift program
3.1.1. The Company matches employee donations to eligible nonprofit organizations, 1:1, up to
$5,000 per employee per calendar year.
3.1.2. Eligible organizations are those qualified as 501(c)(3) public charities in the United States, or the
local equivalent in other countries where the Company has operations (registered charities in the UK,
Canadian Registered Charities, etc.).
3.1.3. Ineligible recipients include political organizations, candidates, religious organizations except
where the funds support a non-religious charitable purpose, and lobbying organizations.
3.1.4. Employees submit match requests through the Bright Funds platform within 60 days of the
donation.
3.2 Volunteer Time Off (VTO)
3.2.1. Full-time employees receive 16 hours of paid Volunteer Time Off per calendar year for
participation in approved volunteer activities.
3.2.2. Part-time employees receive a prorated VTO allocation based on standard hours.
3.2.3. VTO does not accrue and does not carry over year-to-year.

3.2.4. VTO is requested through Workday with the volunteer organization and activity noted.
3.3 Dollars for Doers
3.3.1. Employees who volunteer 25+ hours with an eligible organization in a calendar year receive a
$250 grant for that organization, payable through the matching gift platform.
3.3.2. Employees who volunteer 50+ hours receive a $500 grant.
3.3.3. Multiple grants per year are permitted, capped at $1,000 per employee per year.
3.4 Disaster relief matching
3.4.1. During designated disaster relief events, the Company may enhance the standard match (e.g.,
2:1 instead of 1:1) for donations to approved relief organizations.
3.4.2. Enhanced match events are announced via internal communications and are time-bounded.
3.5 Board service
3.5.1. The Company encourages employees to serve on nonprofit boards as a form of community
engagement.
3.5.2. Reasonable time during business hours for board service may be approved by the employee's
manager (typically up to 8 hours per quarter).
3.5.3. Board service that creates a conflict of interest with the Company's business must be disclosed
under the Code of Conduct (COC-001 §4).
4. Company-led activities
4.1. The Company periodically organizes group volunteer activities, fundraising drives, and
partnerships with nonprofit organizations. Participation in these activities is voluntary.
4.2. Time spent participating in Company-organized activities during normal working hours is paid
and does not count against VTO.
4.3. Company-led activities are coordinated by the Community Engagement team and announced
through internal channels.
5. Eligibility
5.1. Full-time and part-time employees in active status are eligible.
5.2. Contractors and temporary employees are not eligible.
5.3. Eligibility begins on the first day of employment.

6. Tax treatment
6.1. Matching gifts and Dollars-for-Doers grants are made directly by the Company to the recipient
organization. They are not taxable income to the employee.
6.2. VTO is paid time and is taxed as ordinary wages.
6.3. Personal donations are deductible on the employee's individual tax return, subject to applicable
rules.
7. Restrictions
7.1. Charitable activity using Company funds or in a manner that ties the Company's name to a cause
requires Community Engagement team coordination.
7.2. Solicitation of colleagues for personal causes is discouraged but not prohibited; however,
sustained or aggressive solicitation should be avoided.
7.3. Use of Company premises for charitable events requires Facilities approval.
7.4. Political contributions are not eligible for matching, are not VTO-eligible, and are addressed
separately in the Political Activity Policy (LEG-201).
8. Tracking
8.1. Matching gifts, VTO, and Dollars-for-Doers participation are tracked through the Bright Funds
platform and Workday.
8.2. Aggregate community impact is reported annually in the Company's Sustainability Report.
9. Definitions
VTO — Volunteer Time Off.
Eligible organization  — A nonprofit organization that meets the criteria in Section 3.1.2.
Bright Funds  — The Company's matching gift and giving platform vendor.

Business Continuity Plan Summary
Document:  BC-001 Version:  2.0 Effective Date:  January 1, 2025 Owner:  Business Continuity Office
(within Risk Management) Applies To:  All Northwind Logistics business units; this is a public-facing
summary of the full BCP
1. Purpose
Business continuity ensures the Company can maintain or rapidly restore critical operations during
and after a disruption. This summary describes the overall framework; the full Business Continuity
Plan (BCP) and unit-specific runbooks are maintained by the Business Continuity Office and shared
with relevant personnel on a need-to-know basis.
2. Scope
The framework covers all Company operations and critical third-party dependencies. Disruption types
include — but are not limited to — natural disasters, cybersecurity incidents, physical site loss, key-
vendor failure, pandemic, and prolonged staffing disruption.
3. Critical functions
The Company's Business Impact Analysis identifies the following functions as critical, with their target
Recovery Time Objectives (RTO) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPO):
Function RTO RPO
Customer order intake 4 hours 15 minutes
Active shipment tracking 2 hours 5 minutes
Customer support contact (phone, email, chat) 8 hours 1 hour
Payroll processing 5 business days 1 business day
Accounts payable 10 business days 1 business day
Financial reporting (period-close) 5 business days 1 business day
Corporate email and collaboration 4 hours 15 minutes
Non-critical functions have looser RTO targets and are restored after critical functions are stable.

4. Roles
4.1. Business Continuity Office (BCO)  — Maintains the BCP, coordinates exercises, declares plan
activation, reports to executive leadership.
4.2. Crisis Management Team (CMT)  — Activated during major events. Composed of: CEO (or
designated successor), COO, CFO, General Counsel, CHRO, CISO, CIO, Head of Operations.
4.3. Functional Lead  — For each critical function, a named functional lead and at least one backup
lead.
4.4. Crisis Communications Lead  — Coordinates internal and external messaging during a plan
activation. Default: VP, Communications.
5. Activation
5.1. The BCP is activated by the BCO Director or, in their absence, by any member of the Crisis
Management Team.
5.2. Activation triggers: significant disruption affecting one or more critical functions, or anticipated to
affect them within 24 hours.
5.3. Activation is communicated through the Emergency Notification System and via direct outreach
to the CMT.
6. Communication
6.1. Internal communication.  The Emergency Notification System sends initial alerts. Subsequent
updates use a combination of email, internal messaging, and (if available) a dedicated incident page
on intranet.northwindlogistics.com/incident.
6.2. External communication.  Customer communications go through Customer Operations and
Communications jointly. Media communications go through Communications and Legal. Regulator
communications go through Legal.
6.3. Status cadence.  During plan activation, the CMT meets at least daily; functional updates are due
daily by 4 PM local time.
7. Workforce continuity
7.1. The Company's workforce can largely operate remotely. Roles requiring on-site presence
(warehouse, distribution center, certain field operations) have backup-site arrangements.
7.2. During pandemic-style disruptions, remote-first protocols can be activated company-wide.

7.3. Critical roles have designated successors trained on essential procedures.
8. Technology continuity
8.1. Critical systems are deployed in cloud configurations supporting cross-region failover with
documented procedures.
8.2. Backup and recovery testing is conducted quarterly for critical systems and annually for non-
critical.
8.3. Cybersecurity incident response is coordinated by Security per the Incident Response Plan (SEC-
IR-001) and integrated with the BCP for incidents materially affecting operations.
9. Vendor continuity
9.1. Tier A vendors (per PROC-002) have business continuity assessment as part of due diligence.
9.2. Single-source dependencies are tracked by the BCO; mitigation includes contractual SLAs,
backup arrangements, and inventory buffering as appropriate.
10. Site continuity
10.1. The Company maintains an alternate corporate site arrangement (currently Dallas, TX as
alternate to the Irvine HQ).
10.2. Distribution centers have mutual-aid arrangements with sister facilities for re-routing shipments.
11. Exercises and testing
11.1. The BCP undergoes a tabletop exercise at least annually with the CMT.
11.2. Functional teams test their portions of the plan quarterly.
11.3. Full-scale exercises (simulated incident, real workforce response) are conducted biennially.
11.4. Findings from exercises are incorporated into plan updates.
12. Continuous improvement
12.1. After-action reviews follow every plan activation and every significant exercise.
12.2. The BCP is reviewed annually and updated whenever a material change in operations,
technology, or risk profile occurs.

13. Definitions
RTO  — Recovery Time Objective; the targeted duration of time within which a business
function must be restored after disruption.
RPO  — Recovery Point Objective; the maximum acceptable amount of data loss (measured in
time).
BIA — Business Impact Analysis; an assessment of the operational and financial impact of
disruption to each function.
CMT  — Crisis Management Team.
